quotes.quote: We report the detection of a probable ray quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) of around 314 days in the monthly binned 0.1 -- 300 GeV ray Fermi LAT light curve of the well known BL Lac blazar OJ 287. To identify and quantify the QPO nature of the ray light curve of OJ 287, we used the Lomb-Scargle periodogram (LSP), REDFIT, and weighted wavelet z-transform (WWZ) analyses. We briefly discuss possible emission models for radio-loud active galactic nuclei (AGN) that can explain a ray QPO of such a period in a blazar.

OJ 287 z=0.306 ; 1985PASP...97.1158S is a BL Lacartae (BL Lac) object -- a subclass of blazar characterized by very weak or complete absence of emission lines in their optical spectra. The source is famously known for its double-peaked outbursts in optical bands which repeat every 12-yr 1988ApJ...325..628S . Ever since the discovery of OJ 287 VRO 20.08.01; 1967AJ.....72..757D and the identification of an optical counterpart in 1970 1970ApL.....6..201B , it has been a prime target of observations, owing to a variety of observationally favorable properties, including high and correlated multi-wavelength activities and references therein 1970ApL.....6..201B,1985PASP...97.1158S .

sources.summary: The paper tracks OJ 287 after its 2017 VHE activity, finding correlated optical-X-ray variability and spectral changes consistent with a new HBL-like broadband emission component.

quotes.quote: We report the re-emergence of a new broadband emission through a detailed and systematic study of the multi-wavelength spectral and temporal behavior of OJ 287 after its first-ever reported VHE activity in 2017 to date, which includes the second-highest X-ray flux of the source. The source shows high optical to X-ray flux variations, accompanied mainly by strong spectral changes. The optical to X-ray flux variations are correlated and simultaneous except for two durations when they are anti-correlated. The flux variations, however, are anti-correlated with the X-ray spectral state while correlated with optical-UV (ultraviolet).

As per broadband SED, OJ 287 is a low-frequency-peaked/low-synchrotron-peaked (LBL/LSP) blazar with a low-energy peak around near-infrared (NIR) energies and the high-energy peak around 100 MeVs 2010ApJ...716...30A,2013MNRAS.433.2380K .

The observed spectral changes reported here, from optical to X-ray as well as MeV-GeV energies, unambiguously establish the changes to a new HBL like broadband emission component, similar to the one observed during the highest reported X-ray flux and activity state of OJ 287 in 2016--2017 2018MNRAS.479.1672K .

sources.summary: The paper studies OJ 287's 2019-2020 X-ray flare, describing a binary-black-hole blazar/BL Lac at redshift 0.306 with softer-when-brighter behavior.

quotes.quote: OJ 287 is a well studied binary black hole system, that occasionally exhibits bright X-ray and optical flares. Here we present a detailed spectral study of its second brightest X-ray flare observed during 2019-2020 using archival and observations along with ToO observations from . The entire flaring period is divided into three states, defined as low, intermediate, and high state. The variation of hardness ratio (HR) with 0.3-10.0 keV integrated flux suggest a softer-when-brighter behavior, as also previously reported based on flux-index variations.
Blazar OJ 287, having the most massive black hole among all blazars, is located at a redshift 0.306 ( Mao2011 ). It is classified as BL Lac object in the 3FGL ( 3FGL ) 4FGL ( 4FGL ) catalog.

sources.summary: The paper compares OJ287 as a prototypical periodic-quasar and binary-SMBH candidate: a low-redshift blazar with 12-year optical double peaks, X-ray/gamma detections, and strong core-jet radio emission.

quotes.quote: Comparison to Previous Candidate Periodic Quasars OJ287 and PG 1302 102 are two prototypical candidate periodic quasars that have been proposed as candidates for binary SMBHs Lehto1996,valtonen08,Graham2015a . OJ287 is a low-redshift ( z = 0.306 ) blazar Stickel1989 showing candidate double-peaks in its optical light curves every 12 years but see Goyal2018 for evidence against any significant periodicity Sillanpaa1988,Lehto1996 . The periodic peaks since 1891 have been interpreted as arising from the binary motions at similar timescale. OJ287 was detected by the Swift satellite in the X-rays DElia2013 and by the Fermi Large Area Telescope in the rays Fermi_cat4 .

Unlike our targets of DES--SDSS-selected candidate periodic quasars, OJ287 and PG 1302 102 have strong radio emission with flux densities on the order of hundreds of mJy. They share a similar core-jet structure as seen in both the optical and radio observations Hutchings1994,Benitez1996 .

sources.summary: The paper uses OJ 287 optical polarimetry at z=0.306; its fitted coefficient is positive and its data remain consistent with no LIV within the stated confidence level.

quotes.quote: The linear polarization observations of these five blazars are presented in Table tab:observation , including the following information for each object: the energy bands in which polarization is observed; the observed linear polarization angles; and the corresponding uncertainties of the polarization angles. These polarization observations have been obtained simultaneously in five optical bands ( UBVRI ) for all blazars. The redshifts of the five blazars 3C 66A, S5 0716+714, OJ 287, MK 421, and PKS 2155-304 are 0.444, 0.310, 0.306, 0.031, and 0.116, respectively.

As shown in Figure fig:fit , the polarization angles of blazars S5 0716+714 and PKS 2155-304 are proportional to E^2 with a negative coefficient, and the best-fitting values are =(-9.63 4.640) 10^ 8 ( _ 0 =0.320 0.058 rad) and =(-5.32 3.587) 10^ 7 ( _ 0 =1.645 0.018 rad), respectively. While the rest of the blazars show a positive coefficient with the fitting parameters =(3.386 1.249) 10^ 7 ( _ 0 =0.623 0.024 rad), =(6.546 22.769) 10^ 6 ( _ 0 =0.046 0.482 rad), and =(6.226 7.715) 10^ 7 ( _ 0 =1.544 0.094 rad) for 3C 66A, MK 421, and OJ 287, respectively. Our constraints show that except for 3C 66A at the 5.4 confidence level and PKS 2155-304 at the 3.0 confidence level, the data set of the other three blazars are consistent with the possibility of no LIV at all (i.e., = 0 ) within the 1.8 confidence level.

sources.summary: The paper presents RadioAstron and ground VLBI of OJ 287, resolving its innermost jet, finding progressive bending, high Doppler-boosted brightness temperatures, and toroidal or helical magnetic-field evidence.

quotes.quote: We present the first polarimetric space VLBI observations of OJ 287, observed with RadioAstron at 22 GHz during a perigee session on 2014 April 4 and five near-in-time snapshots, together with contemporaneous ground VLBI observations at 15, 43, and 86 GHz. Ground-space fringes were obtained up to a projected baseline of 3.9 Earth diameters during the perigee session, and at a record 15.1 Earth diameters during the snapshot sessions, allowing us to image the innermost jet at an angular resolution of 50 as, the highest ever achieved at 22 GHz for OJ 287. Comparison with ground-based VLBI observations reveals a progressive jet bending with increasing angular resolution that agrees with predictions from a supermassive binary black hole model, although other models cannot be ruled out.

Spectral analyses suggest that the VLBI core is dominated by the internal energy of the emitting particles during the onset of a multi-wavelength flare, while the parsec-scale jet is consistent with being in equipartition between the particles and magnetic field. Estimated minimum brightness temperatures from the visibility amplitudes show a continued rising trend with projected baseline length up to 10^ 13 K, reconciled with the inverse Compton limit through Doppler boosting for a jet closely oriented to the line of sight. The observed electric vector position angle suggests that the innermost jet has a predominantly toroidal magnetic field, which together with marginal evidence of a gradient in rotation measure across the jet width indicate that the VLBI core is threaded by a helical magnetic field, in agreement with jet formation models.

sources.summary: The paper says OJ287 may host a supermassive black hole binary, is historically variable, showed moderate variability early in the monitoring, and entered an active phase by December 2021.

quotes.quote: OJ287 may host a supermassive black hole binary system Dey_et_al_2021 . It is known to be variable and has been monitored at multiple wavelengths for over a century (see e.g. Hudec_et_al_2013, Komossa_et_al_2020, Lister_et_al_2021 and references therein). Our measurements are presented in Figs. fig:fm_OJ287 .

We find OJ287 to exhibit some moderate variability during the first months. However, the significant increase in power observed in our final data points, taken in December 2021, suggest that it has entered an active phase. Continued monitoring of this source at VGOS frequencies could give valuable data for future multi-wavelengths studies.

sources.summary: The paper presents OJ 287 as a binary-SMBH candidate blazar whose NuSTAR observations caught mixed inverse-Compton and synchrotron components during flares.

quotes.quote: OJ 287 OJ 287, also known as 5BZB J0854+2006, is a well-studied blazar at z = 0.306 . It is considered the best candidate AGN for hosting a supermassive binary black hole see e.g. Britzen2018 . Recently, described a low-state Fermi-LAT ray spectrum associated with the kpc scale jet of OJ 287 that is consistent with the predictions of the IC/CMB model for their X-ray emission. However, reported a strong soft X-ray excess in the X-ray spectrum of OJ287 using XMM-Newton observations.

\ observed OJ287 twice during two major flare events detected by Swift (in 2017 and 2020): although the 3.0-10.0 keV X-ray flux was similar ( 3.5 10^ 12 erg cm^ 2 s^ 1 ) during these observations, the photon index varied. It was 2.03 0.04 in 2017 and 2.42 0.06 in 2020.

sources.summary: The paper models OJ 287 as a BL Lac object with an inspiraling SMBH binary whose historical paired flares and 2022 event depend on impacts through the accretion disc.

quotes.quote: However, detailed theoretical investigations and observational campaigns make OJ 287, a BL Lacertae object at a redshift of 0.306 sit85,nil10 , a very special GW induced inspiraling SMBH binary candidate val21 . Interestingly, the binary nature of the OJ 287 central engine was recognised by one of us (Aimo Sillanp a a) already back in 1982, while constructing historical light curves for the quasars in the Tuorla - Mets ahovi variability survey, which had begun two years earlier kid07 . This inference was based on the observational evidence for major flares around 1911, 1923, 1935, 1947, 1959, and 1971 in the historical light curve of OJ 287. From this sequence it was easily extrapolated that OJ 287 should display a major outburst in 1983.

It was recognised soon after that these flares in OJ 287 were not exactly periodic, and that the systematics of the past flares are better understood if the flares come in pairs separated by 1-2 years val96,LV96 . This led to the proposal of a new SMBH binary central engine model for OJ 287, where the secondary SMBH orbits the more massive primary SMBH in a relativistic eccentric orbit with a redshifted orbital period of 12 years.

sources.summary: The paper reports Swift optical-UV-to-X-ray spectra of OJ 287, finding extended synchrotron emission, an unusually hard intrinsic X-ray spectrum, and implications for its jet particle distribution.

quotes.quote: Here, we report the finding of an extended optical-UV spectrum with a power-law photon spectral index of 2.71 0.03 , continuing to the X-ray band during the lowest recorded X-ray flux state of the BL Lacetrae object OJ 287 by the Swift facility. Accounting for the synchrotron contribution at X-rays, we found an X-ray spectrum with a power-law photon spectral index of 1.22 0.22 , the hardest reported X-ray spectrum for the source. The inferred X-ray spectrum is consistent with the spectrum reported at hard energies from the study of the Swift-BAT data. We further found that this X-ray spectrum naturally reproduces most of the flat X-ray spectra when combined with the corresponding optical-UV continuum during the low and intermediate flux states implying synchrotron as the primary driver of most of the X-ray spectral changes in the LBL state of the source.

Combined with sharp-steepening/cutoff of the optical-UV spectrum during bright phases, the extended-spectrum indicates a much larger emission region which may be related to the large-scale jet emission. The optical-UV and X-ray together trace the complete particle distribution required for the observed broadband emission with low and high-energy power-law particle spectral indices of 1.44 0.40 and 4.42 0.06 respectively.

sources.summary: The paper models OJ287 as a reported blazar MBHB candidate at z=0.306 whose merger-ringdown-only signal limits TianQin and LISA parameter estimation.

quotes.quote: Of particular interest are two examples: the extensively studied blazar OJ 287 (OJ287), located at a redshift of z = 0.306 ; and the Seyfert 1-type galaxy SDSS J143016.05 + 230344.4, which hosts a candidate mbhb at redshift of z=0.08105 (referred to `Tick-Tock' hereafter).

Parameter estimation of OJ287 and Tick-Tock In this subsection, we present the constraint results for the OJ287 and Tick-Tock systems, which is presented in Figure fig:OJ287 and Figure fig:Ticktock , respectively. As depicted in Fig. fig:signal , only a portion of the merger and ringdown phases of these two systems can be detected by TianQin and LISA. Thus, this limited detection may have a significant impact on parameter estimation. For the OJ287 system, it can be observed that TianQin's constraint results are slightly looser compared to those of LISA, except for (D_L, , ) . As for the Tick-Tock system, TianQin's constraints for all 11 parameters are looser than those of LISA.

sources.summary: The paper treats OJ287 as a quasar hosting a binary supermassive black-hole system whose optical light curve constrains component masses, orbital precession, eccentricity, and primary spin.

quotes.quote: The most recent application of celestial mechanics is in the binary system of two supermassive black holes in the quasar OJ287. The identification of this system as a likely binary was made as early as 1982 (Sillanp a a et al. 1988), but since the mean period of the system is as long as 12 yr, it has taken a quarter of century to find convincing proof that we are indeed dealing with a binary system (Valtonen et al. 2008). The primary evidence for a binary system comes from the optical light curve.

Most interesting of these are the primary mass of 1.84 10^ 10 M_ , the secondary mass 1.46 10^ 8 M_ , major axis precession rate 39^ .1 per period, and the eccentricity of the orbit 0.70 . The dimensionless spin parameter is 0.28 0.01 (1 sigma).

sources.summary: The paper reports fast high-energy variability from blazar OJ 287, arguing that its emission comes from a relativistic jet launched by the smaller black hole in its supermassive binary.

quotes.quote: We report the discovery of fast variability of ray flares from blazar OJ 287. This blazar is known to be powered by binary system of supermassive black holes. The observed variability time scale T_ var 3-10 hr is much shorter than the light crossing time of more massive ( 1.8 10^ 10 M_ ) black hole and is comparable to the light crossing time of the less massive ( 1.3 10^8M_ ) black hole. This indicates that \ emission is produced by relativistic jet ejected by the black hole of smaller mass. Detection of s with energies in excess of 10 GeV during the fast variable flares constrains the Doppler factor of the jet to be larger than 4.

sources.summary: The paper describes J0854 + 2006 as an LBL with confirmed past INOV, large long-term variability, new two-night light curves, and mixed short-term variability results.

quotes.quote: J0854 + 2006 (OJ 287; z = 0.306 ) This LBL was monitored by Sagar et al. \ (2004) on 4 nights and it showed confirmed INOV each time. It also showed large LTOV, first fading by 0.6 mag over 1 year and then brightening by 1.63 mag on a 2-year time span. In Fig. \ 2 we present the light curves derived from our newly acquired data on 2 nights. The object showed a clear brightening by 7.5 per cent in 4 hours on 12 Apr 2005. However, on 5 Feb 2005, it remained a non-variable (by the C test), although a F- test showed it to be variable.

sources.summary: The paper uses OJ 287 as a VLBI example, resolving its bent core-jet structure and two high-frequency core subcomponents during a rising radio flare after a strong gamma-ray flare.

quotes.quote: As an example we show in Figure 3maps three VLBI maps of the prominent blazar OJ 287 observed in October 2009 at 15 43 GHz with the VLBA, and at 86 GHz with the GMVA. At the lower frequencies, OJ 287 shows a bent core-jet structure, with a prominent and unresolved core and some faint jet-emission extending west to 5 mas. At 86 GHz, however, the core regions breaks up into two bright sub-components (C1 C2), which interestingly are oriented along a line almost perpendicular to the direction of the mas-scale jet. If interpreted by a spatially bent jet, one would expect that the northern most component (C1) to be the VLBI core, which is the unresolved base of a synchrotron self-absorbed jet.
In October 2009, OJ 287 was in the rising phase of a prominent radio-flare (peak: Jan. 2010), which followed a strong gamma-ray flare (peak: October 24, 2009, see ).

sources.summary: The review highlights OJ 287 as the BL Lac with a 12-year optical brightening cycle, interpreted through a binary black-hole model with large outbursts and shorter quasi-periodic variations.

quotes.quote: The only exception is OJ 287 ( z = 0.306 ), for which a regular flux brightening recurrence every 12 years was reported silla1988b,takalo1994 and confirmed by the detection of 9 equally spaced maxima - of which the latest 4 are very well sampled - in the optical light curve so far see Fig. oj287lc ; sillanpaa1996,hudec2013,pihajoki2013a . The period is ascribed to the presence of a binary system of massive black holes at the center of the nucleus, that produce outbursts, often accompanied by precursors, when approaching pericenter valtonen2008 . In these occasions the flux may brighten by more than a factor of 100 with respect to quiescence. Shorter-term variations with periodic and quasi-periodic character were also detected, that may be related to the matter dynamics in the close vicinity of the binary black hole system pihajoki2013b . On the other hand, suggest maxima at times that are not foreseen by the model, making the interpretative picture not completely satisfactory.

sources.summary: The paper describes OJ287 as a BL Lac object thought to host a binary black hole, where VLBI core shifts and jet-angle changes may trace precession or orbital motion.

quotes.quote: The BL Lac object OJ287, for instance, is believed to host a BBH system for its nearly 12 years periodic optical/radio outbursts that attributing to the orbital motion of BBH. The VLBI image of OJ287 shows only one-sided jet, exhibiting large position-angle changes of inner-jets at high frequencies and with the core-shifts between different frequencies in VLBI images as well. The core shifts are mainly caused by the opacity effect of inner jets. The position-angle changes of inner jets, however, may be caused by the precession or orbital motion of the BBH system. Therefore, with multi-epoch VLBI observations at same frequency, one would be able to detect the orbital motion of the radio loud BH in the BBH system if the other is radio quiet, the so-called `core offset', with the phase-reference VLBI technique (see Fig.  fig1 ), as referenced to a distant high-redshift quasar (which has no detectable core-offset).

sources.summary: The paper adopts a lower accretion-luminosity limit and black-hole mass for OJ 287, notes binary black-hole mass estimates, and corrects a proton jet-power typo.

quotes.quote: B13 quote an estimate of L_ acc of xie08 for OJ 287, which is 7 times higher than the upper limit of G10; here we use the latter.

For OJ 287, we use 6.2 10^8 from wang04 . However, if the system is indeed a binary black-hole system, the mass of the less and more massive component is estimated as 1.4 10^ 8 and 1.8 10^ 10 , respectively valtonen12 . Accretion would be dominated by the more massive black hole.

B13 give the jet power The entry for L_ p (the power in protons) of OJ 287 in B13 is a typo, it should be 0.083 rather than 8.3. estimated using the energy content and for one jet only, see their equations (3--5).

sources.summary: The review presents OJ287 as the best-studied periodically variable blazar, with a century-long optical light curve modeled as a precessing SMBH binary.

quotes.quote: The best studied such case is the blazar OJ287. Its optical lightcurve has been observed for more than a century (thanks to photographic plate archives all around the world, and intense dedicated monitoring during the last few decades) and shows repeat outbursts every 12 yrs (e.g., Sillanp a a et al. 1988, Valtaoja et al. 2000, Valtonen et al. 2012), each of which is composed of two peaks separated by 1 yr. The best explored SMBBH model consists of a secondary BH in a precessing orbit, which impacts a warped accretion disk around the primary twice each orbit. Precise timing of the past optical peaks then allows to derive the orbital parameters of the system. Calculating Keplerian orbits with post-Newtonian corrections, Valtonen (2007) presented an orbital solution with a primary mass of 2 10 ^ 10 M _ , a mass ratio 0.01, an eccentricity of =0.66 , and a semi-major axis of 0.045 pc.

sources.summary: The paper presents PKS 0851 + 202, also OJ 287, as a well-known AGN/FSRQ with periodic optical behavior, multiwavelength variability, and strong activity in both monitored filters.

quotes.quote: PKS 0851 + 202: (also known as OJ 287) is a well known AGN. Its redshift is well-determined at z=0.306 Nilsson10 , and it is usually classified as a FSRQ Healey07 . OJ 287 shows a periodical behaviour in its optical emission. Analysing its historical light-curve, several studies concluded that it shows a period of 12 years Sillanpaa88,Sillanpaa96 , explained by a binary black hole system Sillanpaa88,Valtaoja00,Valtonen08 . Variability has also been detected both at radio and X-ray wavelengths Urry96,Hughes98,Hovatta08 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies OJ 287 as a famous BL Lac/blazar hosting a supermassive binary black hole, with strong polarization and occasional rapid EVPA rotations lasting 10-25 days.

quotes.quote: OJ 287 OJ 287 (z=0.305) is a BL Lac object and one of the most famous blazars as it hosts a supermassive binary black hole system at its centre sillanpaa1988 . It is bright in HE rays acciari2009 but has not been detected in VHE rays (e.g. 2009PASJ...61.1011S ). The dedicated studies of the optical polarisation behaviour 2009ApJ...697..985D, 2010MNRAS.402.2087V, uemura2010 have shown that there is a strong preferred position angle for the polarisation which is perpendicular to the flow of the jet. The polarisation is strong (maximum 35 ). Occasionally the EVPA also shows rapid rotations with durations of 10-25 days.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes a 9.2-year optical light curve of BL Lac OJ 287, reporting possible 400- and 800-day quasi-periodic signals and favoring modulation by magnetized accretion disks.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we present the results of our time series analysis of 9.2 year-long, and exceptionally well-sampled optical light curve of the BL Lac OJ 287. The study primarily uses the data from our own observations performed at the Mt. Suhora and Krak ow Observatories in Poland, and at the Athens Observatory in Greece. Additionally, SMARTS observations were used to fill in some of the gaps in the data. The Lomb-Scargle Periodogram and the Weighted Wavelet Z-transform methods were employed to search for the possible QPOs in the resulting optical light curve of the source. Both the methods consistently yielded possible quasi-periodic signal around the periods of 400 and 800 days, the former one with a significance (over the underlying colored noise) of 99 . A number of likely explanations for such are discussed, with a preference given to a modulation of the jet production efficiency by highly magnetized accretion disks. This supports the previous findings and the interpretation reported recently in the literature for OJ 287 and other blazar sources.

sources.summary: The paper discusses OJ287 as an LSP BL Lac with 12-year optical periodicity, binary-black-hole interpretation, jet wobbling, and prominent radio and optical intra-day variability.

quotes.quote: OJ287, 2FGL J 0854.8+2005 (LSP BL) The BL Lac object is known for its 12-year periodicity in the optical light curve silla88 . The optical outbursts appear as double peaked and their periodic occurrence is interpreted primarily in terms of a binary black hole system valt07 . The model predicted a major optical outburst in December, 2015, which occurred within the expected time range, peaking on December 5, 2015 at a magnitude of 12.9 in the optical R band (Valtonen et al. 2016). Various time scales and periods (or quasi-periodic oscillations) have been reported in the optical band sand16,bhat16,zola16 . In the radio band, it shows a one-sided core jet on the kpc scale to the west, similar to its VLBI jet direction per94 .

sources.summary: The paper describes OJ 287 as a blazar whose XMM-Newton pointings alternate between simple power-law fits and significant negative curvature with 1.5--3.0 keV breaks.

quotes.quote: OJ 287: The X-ray spectrum of this blazar is analyzed by Donato et al. (2005) in two occasions and found the spectra were well characterized by simple power law with =1.6--1.9. Massaro et al. (2003) analyzed the X-ray spectra of this source during its optical bright state in 2001 and found the spectrum to be described by power law model. Seta et al. (2009) analyzed Suzaku observations of the blazar in April and November 2007, in quiescent and flaring states, respectively. They found the simple power law to describe the spectra with photon indices =1.65 and 1.50 in quiescent and flaring states, respectively. In more recent studies Siejkowski Wierzcholska (2017) found flat spectrum of OJ 287, which can be interpreted as an indication of the spectral upturn located in the X-ray regime. We analyzed five pointings of XMM Newton and found one observation in April 2005 and April 2008 to be well fitted by simple power law. Other three pointings in November 2005, November 2006 and October 2011 have shown significant negative spectral curvature with break energy varying between 1.5--3.0 keV.

sources.summary: The paper models OJ 287 as a blazar with a supermassive black-hole binary, testing hadronic emission from an impact outflow to explain its November 2015 multiwavelength flare.

quotes.quote: A super-massive black hole (SMBH) binary in the core of the blazar OJ 287 has been invoked in previous works to explain its observed optical flare quasi-periodicity. Following this picture, we investigate a hadronic origin for the X-ray and ray counterparts of the November 2015 major optical flare of this source. An impact outflow must result after the lighter SMBH (the secondary) crosses the accretion disc of the heavier one (the primary). We then consider acceleration of cosmic-ray (CR) protons in the shock driven by the impact outflow as it expands and collides with the active galactic nucleus (AGN) wind of the primary SMBH. We show that the emission of these CRs can reproduce the X-ray and ray flare data self-consistently with the optical component of the November 2015 major flare.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes OJ 287 as a BL Lac blazar with century-long optical variability, binary black-hole outburst models, and pre-outburst optical, UV, and X-ray spectral variability from jet-related components.

quotes.quote: The BL Lacertae (BL Lac) object OJ 287 (0851 + 203; z = 0.306) is one of the brightest and most frequently observed blazar. OJ 287 shares the common BL Lac properties of blazar class which include significant flux variation in the complete electromagnetic (EM) spectrum on diverse timescales, variable radio to optical polarization, superluminal motion of the compact radio core, and predominantly non-thermal emission (e.g., and references therein). Blazars emit relativistic charged particles in the form of collimated jets pointing towards the observer's line of sight 1995PASP..107..803U , and their radiation covers a wide range of frequencies giving us an excellent opportunity to study their broadband spectral energy distributions (SEDs) which can help us to understand the hidden physical scenario in their local environment.

In the present work, we focus on temporal and spectral evolution of the blazar in the pre-outburst period shown between the two vertical lines in Figure fig1 . The X-ray light curve of the blazar OJ 287 was generated using observations between June 2018 to June 2020 and is shown in Figure fig1 , along with the UV--optical light curves from June 2018 to March 2020.

sources.summary: The paper detects the host galaxy of OJ 287 in optical and near-infrared imaging, finding early-type host photometry and an overmassive central black hole.

quotes.quote: The BL Lacertae object OJ 287 (z = 0.306) has unique double-peaked optical outbursts every 12 years, and it presents one of the best cases for a small-separation binary supermassive black hole (SMBH) system, with an extremely massive primary (M_ BH /M_ ) 10.3. However, the host galaxy is unresolved or only marginally detected in all optical studies so far, indicating a large deviation from the bulge mass - SMBH mass relation. We have obtained deep, high spatial resolution i-band and K-band images of OJ 287 when the target was in a low state, which enable us to detect the host galaxy. We find the broad-band photometry of the host to be consistent with an early type galaxy with M_ R = -22.5 and M_ K = -25.2 , placing it in the middle of the host galaxy luminosity distribution of BL Lacertae objects. The central supermassive black hole is clearly overmassive for a host galaxy of that luminosity, but not unprecedented, given some recent findings of other ``overmassive'' black holes in nearby galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper models photon-ALP propagation from OJ 287's BL Lac jet and identifies it as a strong X-ray and high-energy target for polarization tests.

quotes.quote: In particular, by closely following the analysis developed in  , we investigate the photon-ALP beam propagation for photons emitted at the jet base of several BL Lacs: OJ 287, BL Lacertae, Markarian 501 and 1ES 0229+200 and we compute -- for each of them -- the photon survival probability P_ and the corresponding final photon degree of linear polarization _L and polarization angle . By using the state-of-the-art knowledge concerning the crossed regions (blazar jet, host galaxy, extragalactic space, Milky Way) and physically consistent bounds on the photon-ALP system parameters, we conclude that ALPs induce sizable effects on the final photon polarization which is observable by present and planned missions  . In particular, OJ 287 and BL Lacertae appear as good observational targets both in the X-ray and in the HE band, 1ES 0229+200 in the X-ray range only, while Markarian 501 does not represent a good candidate for studies of ALP effects on photon polarization.

OJ 287 is a low-frequency peaked blazar (LBL) observed at redshift z=0.3056 . It is considered a good observational target for polarimetric studies both in the X-ray and HE range because of the high flux in both these energy bands  .

sources.summary: The paper explains why OJ287 is excluded from the periodic-source list: its estimated radio period exceeds the time-coverage criterion, though possible long and residual 1.6-year modulations are detected.

quotes.quote: It may look surprising that our list of sources with evidence for periodicity does not include the most famous case, OJ287. The reason is that the estimated period does not meet our criterion of being shorter than 1/2 of the time coverage. In fact, we detect a possible modulation on a period of about 21--22 years at14.5, 22, and 37 GHz, and on a longer period (about 25 years) at 4.8 GHz (see Fig. OJ287 ). These possible periodicities are about a factor of 2 larger than found by analyses of optical data (Sillanp a a et al. 1988, 1996; Pietil a et al. 1999). An analysis of the residuals (after having subtracted the sinusoidal modulation) detects a significant periodicity of 1.6 yr at 22, 37, and 14.5, in close agreement with that found by Aller et al. (1992) using a Scargle periodogram and by Hughes et al. (1998) using a wavelet transform.

sources.summary: The paper describes OJ 287 as the clearest periodic-variability binary-supermassive-black-hole candidate, with 11.86-year double-peaked optical outbursts and models invoking a secondary black hole.

quotes.quote: Undoubtedly the clearest example is OJ 287, a ``blazar,'' i.e. an active galaxy in which the jet is believed to be orientated nearly parallel to the line of sight, at z=0.306 . Optical variability of OJ 287 has been recorded since 1890 and has a strict period of 11.86 yr ( 9 yr in the galaxy's rest frame); the last major outburst was observed (on schedule) in 1994. The outbursts are generally double-peaked with the peaks separated by about a year; the second peak is accompanied by enhanced radio emission. Models to explain the periodicity usually invoke a second SBH with q 0.1 . In one class of model, the variability reflects true changes in the source luminosity due to variations in the accretion rate as the smaller SBH passes through the accretion disk surrounding the larger SBH .

sources.summary: The paper identifies OJ 287 as a source with gamma-ray and radio activity, where EVPA behavior during an outburst is consistent with an oblique shock.

quotes.quote: Activity, including strong flaring, occurred in both the ray and radio wavebands. Figure fig3 shows both the ray and radio band light curves for OJ 287. In this source the signature of a shock temporally associated with the outburst observed by Fermi is clearly apparent. The swing in EVPA through a limited range of about 40 is consistent with the passage of an oblique shock.

sources.summary: OJ 287 has scattered two-part color behavior, slight redder-when-brighter behavior in Color-J, previously reported bluer-when-brighter behavior, and circular color-magnitude motion.

quotes.quote: OJ 287 indicates two parts in the color behavior, and the data points are very scattered. The color seems to be no trend in the Color-R diagram, however, show a slight RWB trend in the Color-J diagram. The BWB trend had been found in this source vagne03,fioru04,wu06,dai11 . A tendency to move around a circle in Color-Magnitude diagram was also found by and .

sources.summary: The paper maps OJ 287's relativistic jet polarization, tying optical synchrotron emission to the core and a downstream stationary feature where shocks accelerate energetic electrons.

quotes.quote: We analyze the linear polarization of the relativistic jet in BL Lacertae object OJ 287 as revealed by multi-epoch Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA) images at 43 GHz and monitoring observations at optical bands. The electric-vector position angle (EVPA) of the optical polarization matches that at 43 GHz at locations that are often in the compact millimeter-wave ``core'' or, at other epochs, coincident with a bright, quasi-stationary emission feature 0.2 milliarcsec ( 0.9 pc projected on the sky) downstream from the core. This implies that electrons with high enough energies to emit optical synchrotron and ray inverse Compton radiation are accelerated both in the core and at the downstream feature, the latter of which lies 10 pc from the central engine. The polarization vector in the stationary feature is nearly parallel to the jet axis, as expected for a conical standing shock capable of accelerating electrons to GeV energies.

sources.summary: OJ 287 is described as a well-studied BL Lac with violent optical variability; this campaign detected significant INOV, a short flare, and high polarization.

quotes.quote: J0854.8+2006: OJ 287 is one of the best studied BL Lac objects in our sample and is known to show violent optical variability see, e.g., 2009ApJS..181..466F,2011MNRAS.416..101G . We have observed this source on 2015 March 10 and a significant INOV was detected. In particular, the source was found to be in a relatively moderate ray activity state and we noted a small amplitude ( 0.05 mag) flare that lasted less than an hour (see Figure fig_inov3 ). The optical polarimetric monitoring from Steward observatory on 2015 March 17, closest to the epoch of our observation, reveals the detection of a high polarization of 13.4 (Table tab:stat ), thus indicating the synchrotron based origin of the INOV.

sources.summary: The paper describes OJ 287 as a BL Lac object whose dense VLBI monitoring traces 17 radio knots, including stationary features interpreted as jet rotation or nutation.

quotes.quote: For another prominent BL Lac object, OJ 287, the recently collated exceptionally dense VLBI monitoring data are particularly revealing. Analysis of 120 VLBI observations of OJ 287 at 15 GHz, over the period 1995-2017, has enabled tracking the proper motions of its 17 radio knots, out of which 3 are found to be `stationary' (i.e., maintaining a constant separation from the core), while the remaining 14 knots exhibit steady outward motions (Fig. 8). Tracing of the motion in the 2-dimensional space, however, reveals that even the `stationary' knots move . Modelling of their motions (Britzen et al. \ 2018) shows that they may well be tracing the nuclear relativistic jet's rotation/nutation on time scale of 1 year.

sources.summary: The paper includes OJ 287 among extremely massive SMBH systems used to constrain light bosons, citing its dynamical black-hole mass and spin and contribution to exclusion bands.

quotes.quote: There are some even more massive black holes. OJ 287 has the dynamically measured mass M_ bh =(1.8348 0.0008) 10^ 10 M _ and spin a_ * =0.381 0.004 . The masses of Ton 618 and SDSS J140821.67+025733.2 are estimated to be 6.6 10^ 10 M _ and 1.96 10^ 11 M _ , respectively. The allowed parameter space of the BH mass and spin within the thin disc accretion model has been examined in Ref. , giving lower limits of the spin of about 0.6 for Ton 618 and 0.97 for SDSS J140821.67+025733.2. These SMBHs will also be included in this study.

The orange (blue) bands are those for the most massive SMBHs OJ 287, Ton 618, and SDSS J140821.67+025733.2. Our results extend remarkably the constraints compared with earlier approaches . Panel (b) of Fig. scalar_vector summarizes the combined exclusion mass regions for all the SMBHs used in this work. We can see that the mass range of 10^ 22 10^ 17 eV can be effectively constrained by these sources, particularly for the vector boson scenario due to its faster superradiance rate compared with the scalar scenario (see Eq. ( eq:4 ) and Eq. ( eq:5 )).

sources.summary: The paper models OJ 287 as a BL Lac object, choosing its LBL phase for broadband SED fits and estimating high jet power in the synchrotron self-Compton scenario.

quotes.quote: We modeled five different SEDs of blazars, three for BL Lac object, one each for OJ 287 (z = 0.306), S5 0716+714 (z = 0.31) and PKS 2155-304 (z = 0.116), and the rest two for FSRQ 3C 454.3 (z = 0.859). The BLL OJ 287 has exhibited diverse spectral phases, from LBL to LBL+HBL . For BLLs broadband SEDs, we have taken LBL phase of OJ 287, IBL S5 0716+714 , and HBL PKS 2155-304 .

For BLL the jet power of best modeled SED for OJ 287 (left panel of figure fig:sscOj287a b), S5 0716+714 (middle panel of figure fig:sscOj287a b) and PKS 2155-304 (right panel of figure fig:sscOj287a a) are 3.3 10^ 52 , 6.8 10^ 52 and 1.0 10^ 53 erg/s respectively.

sources.summary: The paper uses OJ287 as a blazar with century-scale jet observations, a 20 yr precession period, possible binary-black-hole driving, and a modeled LISA signal outside the fiducial horizon.

quotes.quote: A classic example is the ``Rosetta Stone'' blazar OJ287, whose jet has been observed for 100+ years and is precessing over a timescale of 20 yrs , possibly driven by the evolution of a BH binary .

OJ287, the seminal blazar, has distance z = 0.306 , binary BH mass M = 4e8 , and t_ obs 20 yrs , implying that a primary BH spin aligned from accretion provides t_ tid 50 yrs for a binary separation = r_ decoup 0.006 pc. The corresponding LISA SNR is 0 due to the high mass and modest redshift placing it just outside the LISA horizon (see e.g., Fig. F:waterfall ), although a smaller instrumental lower frequency cut-off would make it observable, e.g. f_ low = 10^ 5 Hz as in .
However there is some evidence of a binary BH scenario for OJ287 and 2MAS .

sources.summary: The paper measures weak negative 15 GHz core circular polarization in OJ287, compares nondetections at earlier VLBA epochs, and cites older integrated detections.

quotes.quote: 0851 + 202 (OJ287): We measure m_c = -0.20 0.08 in the core at 15 GHz in October of 2002. HW99 did not detect core circular polarization at 15 GHz at any epoch during 1996, with a typical limit of |m_c| < 0.2 . HAW01 also found a limit of |m_c| < 0.10 for the core circular polarization at 5 GHz in December of 1996. In older integrated measurements during the period 1978--1983, AAP03 found strong CP at 4.8 and 8.0 GHz with m_c = -0.37 0.12 and m_c = -0.22 0.03 respectively.

sources.summary: The paper treats OJ 287 as an intermediate-redshift blazar whose optical/GeV flux point sits between two observed blazar regimes rather than inside either cluster.

quotes.quote: For OJ 287 we get the single isolated point roughly in the middle of the two cluster of points.
Once more OJ 287 falls in the middle of these two regimes with a z 0.3 .
An intermediate redshift source OJ 287 is found straddling the region in between these regimes with a redshift of z=0.3 .

sources.summary: The paper reports that OJ 287 lacked a global long-term color-magnitude correlation but showed chromatism in shorter intervals and large optical flux variations.

quotes.quote: OJ 287: The source was observed during 148 nights in 2007--2012. No global CM correlations can be noted in the longterm lightcurve, although chromatism can be seen in shorter isolated intervals, in agreement with the previous results presented in the literature e.g., Carini1992,Dai,Ikejiri2011 . The source displayed large, 2 mag flux variations and spectral indices from _ BR 2 to 3.

sources.summary: The paper models OJ 287 as a BL Lac blazar needing oblique shocks and a very low cutoff energy, constraining its polarized flare light curves.

quotes.quote: The selected sources were the QSO 0420-014, the BL Lac object OJ 287, and the FSRQ 1156+295.

We found that the general structure and spectrum of both the total and polarized flux light curves could be reproduced with forward moving shocks, preferentially oriented transversely to the flow direction, with OJ 287 being distinct in needing a significantly oblique shock structure, and very low cutoff energy for the radiating particle distribution.

The discussion of Section lowenergycutoff shows that very low values of the cutoff are excluded: in only one case (that of OJ 287) is the cutoff low enough for its effects to be clearly evident in the polarized flux light curves, but, even there, the cutoff is at highly relativistic energy; for the other sources, the absence of evidence for such Faraday effects in the data constrain the cutoff to be even higher.

sources.summary: The paper describes OJ 287 as a heavily studied LBL with twelve-year optical periodicity, observed by VERITAS during its 2007 active phase and again in 2010.

quotes.quote: OJ 287 (LBL, z=0.306 ) This blazar is one of the most studied objects of its kind due to a clear periodicity in its optical lightcurve, with a period of about twelve years. The \ observations presented in this work cover the last active phase in Fall 2007 (from December 4, 2007 to January 1, 2008), with additional observations during 2010. The VHE upper limits are comparable to the ones measured with the \ telescopes and presented by OJ287 .

sources.summary: The review describes OJ 287 as a newly reported VHE blazar detected during a high X-ray state, with 12-year optical outbursts suggesting a binary supermassive black hole in its core.

quotes.quote: During this ICRC, the detection of four more blazars was reported--OJ 287 and RGB J2056+496 by VERITAS, PKS 0736+017 by H.E.S.S., and OT 081 by MAGIC.

OJ 287 was detected in 2017 during a historically high state observed in the X-ray band. This source shows 12 year periodic outbursts in the optical band, indicating that there may be a binary supermassive black hole system in its core.

sources.summary: OJ 287 is presented as a luminous, rapidly variable BL Lac object whose X-ray data are modeled with several spectral forms and show log-normal count-rate behavior.

quotes.quote: OJ 287 This source is one of the most luminous and rapidly variable BL Lacs at radio to optical frequencies 1989A&AS...80..103S .
When studying the spectra of OJ 287, we also used a fourth model -- an addition of black-body and log-parabolic with peak energy (BBEL).
OJ 287 Our study was conducted using eight XMM-Newton observations which have been held from April 2005 to April 2020.

sources.summary: The paper identifies this well-known LSP BL Lac, monitored it on three nights, and detected about two percent INOV on one night.

quotes.quote: 3FGL J0854.8+2007: It is a well known BL Lac and belongs to the LSP type. It was observed on 25 February 2017 and again in February and March 2018. It showed INOV on one of the three nights of observations (20 March 2018) with an amplitude of variability of about 2 .

sources.summary: OJ 287 is presented as the best-known supermassive binary black hole candidate, with a 10^10 solar-mass primary, 10^8 solar-mass secondary, 12-year orbit, and space-ground interferometry prospects.

quotes.quote: The best-known candidate for a SMBBH is the OJ 287 . According to the most preferred model, the mass of the main component is 10^ 10 M_ , the mass of the secondary component is 10^8 M_ , and the orbital period in the binary system is 12 years. The large (as compared to other known SMBH) angular size of the more massive component shadow, 0.2 s , and the total flux on the order of Jy make it a good candidate for observations with the space-ground interferometer .

gmw_00000432.name: Ton 599
gmw_00000432.overview: Ton 599 is a highly variable flat-spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of approximately 0.725 [arXiv:2112.06681, arXiv:2110.13181]. It features a core-dominated radio morphology with a curved, oscillating parsec-scale jet that exhibits superluminal motion [arXiv:1102.3046, arXiv:0809.2943].

sources.summary: The paper studies 1156 + 295 as a radio-loud flat-spectrum quasar with a compact core-jet structure, fast jet components, and oscillating parsec-scale morphology best explained by a Kelvin-Helmholtz helical mode.

quotes.quote: 1156 + 295 is a flat-spectrum quasar that is loud at both radio and ray wavelengths. Previous observations of the source revealed a radio morphology on pc to kpc scales consistent with a helical jet model. We carried out VLBA observations at centimeter and millimeter wavelengths to study the structure of the innermost jet and understand the relation between the helical structure and astrophysical processes in the central engine.

A core-jet structure with six jet components is identified in 1156 + 295. Three jet components are detected for the first time. The apparent transverse velocities of the six jet components derived from proper motion measurements range between 3.6 and 11.6 , suggesting that highly relativistic jet plasma moves in the direction close to the line of sight. The overall jet has an oscillating morphology with multiple curvatures on pc scales, which might be indicative of a helical pattern. Models of a helical jet are discussed in the context of both Kelvin-Helmholtz (K-H) instability and jet precession.

sources.summary: The paper monitors quasar 1156+295 at 4.8 GHz, finding prominent intra-day variability whose timescales favor anisotropic interstellar scintillation, plus a 2009 flare investigated through VLBA core evolution.

quotes.quote: The source 1156+295 (4C +29.45, with redshift of 0.724488, and Galactic coordinates l=199.41 , b=+78.37 ) is a flat-spectrum radio quasar, and also a Fermi ray source. It is one of the targets of our monitoring campaign and was observed nearly on a monthly basis from October 2007 to October 2009. In this paper, we present the results of the IDV observations of 1156+295, analyze the variability timescales and discuss possible effects of the source flare that peaked around March 2009 on the IDV.

We have carried out nearly monthly IDV observations of quasar 1156+295 in two years, with the Urumqi 25m radio telescope at 4.8 GHz, and the source has shown prominent IDV in most observing sessions. We estimate the IDV timescale with the method of structure function analysis. The IDV timescales stacking through the day of year can be best-fitted with an anisotropic ISS model, and although the data are relatively sparse, the result is positive in favor of an annual modulation of the IDV timescales, suggesting at least a significant part of the flux density variations are due to ISS. The source underwent a dramatic flare peaking around March 2009 at 4.8 GHz. We analyzed the flux density and size evolution of the model-fitted VLBA core component at 43 GHz to find possible influences on the IDV of the source.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1156+295 as a variable blazar/quasar whose 2010 gamma-ray flares are tied to superluminal jet components and radio-core activity.

quotes.quote: The blazar 1156+295 was active at ray energies, exhibiting three prominent flares during the year 2010. Here, we present results using the combination of broadband (X-ray through mm single dish) monitoring data and radio band imaging data at 43 GHz on the connection of ray events to the ejections of superluminal components and other changes in the jet of 1156+295. The kinematics of the jet over the interval 2007.0--2012.5 using 43 GHz Very Long Baseline Array observations, reveal the presence of four moving and one stationary component in the inner region of the blazar jet. The propagation of the third and fourth components in the jet corresponds closely in time to the active phase of the source in rays.

This quasar, located at redshift z = 0.729 Veron2010 , displays strong variability across the electromagnetic spectrum. Prior to Fermi /LAT observations, the source was detected at ray energies only in the second EGRET catalogue Thompson1995 . However, the Fermi /LAT with its better sensitivity, had already detected 1156+295 at 1.6 10^ 7 after only three months of operation Abdo2009 . This object is classified as an optically violent variable and highly polarized quasar cf. Wills1983, Wills1992, Fan2006 .

sources.summary: The paper studies Ton 599 as a redshift 0.725 FSRQ/quasar, measuring Fermi-LAT gamma-ray variability across quiescent, pre-flaring, and main-flaring epochs in 2020-2021.

quotes.quote: Ton 599 (4FGL J1159.5 + 2914; 2020ApJS..247...33A ) is an FSRQ, located at redshift z = 0.725 2010MNRAS.405.2302H with R.A. = 11^ h 59^ m 31.8^ s and Dec. = +29^ 14 ^ 43.8 " . Ton 599 is a strongly polarized and a highly optically violent variable quasar 2006PASJ...58..797F . It was first detected in high energy rays by the Energetic Gamma Ray Experiment Telescope (EGRET) in the second EGRET catalogue 1995ApJS..101..259T and later by the Fermi Large Area Telescope ( Fermi LAT) 2010ApJ...715..429A . The correlation study between radio and ray bands was carried out by 2014MNRAS.445.1636R for this source to put the constraints on ray emission region in parsec-scale jets.

For all the three epochs of the source Ton 599, we estimated the flux variability amplitude using one-day binned light curves. We found that during the main-flaring epoch (epoch C), the source showed largest variations with F_ var = 0.35 0.03. During the pre-flaring epoch (epoch B), the source was also variable with F_ var = 0.22 0.04 and during the quiescent epoch (epoch A), the source was not significantly variable ( F_ var = 0.36 0.12) within the error bar. During the main-flare (epoch C), the source showed a maximum flux of 2.24 0.25 10^ 6 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 at MJD 59,399.50, which is larger than the maximum flux observed during the 2017 flare .

sources.summary: The paper identifies 1156+295 as a variable flat-spectrum radio quasar at z=0.729 and reports serendipitous 15 GHz VLBA intraday correlated-flux variations as high as 40 over 2.7 hours.

quotes.quote: We report a serendipitous detection of rapid, large amplitude flux density variations in the highly core-dominated, flat-spectrum radio quasar 1156+295 during an observing session at the Very Long Baseline Array (VLBA). The source was observed as a part of the MOJAVE survey programme with the VLBA at 15 GHz on February 5, 2007. Large amplitude variability in the correlated flux density, unexplainable in terms of the source structure, was first discovered while processing the data, and later confirmed by calibrating the antenna gains using 24 other sources observed in the experiment. The source shows variations in the correlated flux density as high as 40 on a timescale of only 2.7 hours.

1156+295 ( 4C +29.45 ) is an optically violently variable quasar at z=0.729 bur68 , which has Galactic coordinates l=199.4 , b=+78.4 . The source is strongly variable throughout the electromagnetic spectrum from radio to rays. At radio frequencies, 1156+295 shows significant long-term variability on a timescale of months e.g. kov02 .

sources.summary: The paper reports Ton 599 as a distant FSRQ detected in a high state by VERITAS and MAGIC, with soft VHE spectrum and roughly 12 percent Crab flux.

quotes.quote: Ton 599 is an FSRQ at redshift z = 0.7247 . It is a known, variable MeV-GeV ray emitter, but its Fermi LAT spectrum ( _ 3FGL = 2.1 ) softens considerably at higher energy. It does not appear in the 2FHL ( > 50 GeV) catalog, and the extrapolation of its 3FHL ( > 10 GeV) spectrum ( _ 3FHL = 3.0 ) to the VHE band (F( > 200 GeV) 0.2 Crab flux) suggests it is not normally detectable using VERITAS. Given its large redshift and unfavorable high-energy characteristics it was not observed by VERITAS prior to reports of a remarkably high state in the optical, infrared, X-ray and ray bands.

On December 15 and 16, 2017, an excess of rays, corresponding to a statistical significance of 10 , was quickly ( 2 h) detected from the direction of Ton 599 (ATel 11075). The observed VHE flux is 12 Crab and the spectrum is very soft ( 5 ). The object was detected on the same night (December 15) by MAGIC (ATel 11061) and was hence co-discovered at VHE by the two projects.

sources.summary: The paper describes Ton 599 as a VHE-detected FSRQ whose 2017 flare SED implies a very high Doppler factor and parsec-scale gamma-ray emission region.

quotes.quote: Finally, Ton 599, at z=0.725 ( Schneider2010 ; see also Burbidge1968 ) and also known as 4C +29.45 and B1156+295, entered a months-long GeV high state in October 2017 Cheung2017 , leading to VHE detections on the nights of December 15 and 16 2017 2017ATel11061....1M, 2017ATel11075....1M .

Using observations from VERITAS as well as Fermi , Swift , and the Steward Observatory, we model the broadband spectral energy distributions of PKS 1222+216 and Ton 599 during VHE-detected flares in 2014 and 2017, respectively, strongly constraining the jet Doppler factors and gamma-ray emission region locations during these events. Finally, we place theoretical constraints on the potential production of PeV-scale neutrinos during these VHE flares.

The SEDs of VHE flares of PKS 1222+216 and Ton 599 are described well by a purely leptonic emission model including an external inverse Compton emission component. For both sources, a strong constraint was placed on the Doppler factor, which must be 40 for PKS 1222+216 and 50 for Ton 599, to produce the observed gamma-ray emission at up to TeV energies despite internal absorption. We constrained the jet Lorentz factor and distance of the gamma-ray emission region from the central black hole using the independent method of Nalewajko2014 , which we augmented using an opacity constraint derived from our SED modeling. We found that both sources are operating in a similar regime, with both of them having strongly matter-dominated energetics and a gamma-ray emission zone located a few parsecs from the SMBH.

sources.summary: The paper studies the blazar Ton 599 during its 2017 flaring state, reporting multi-band variability, polarization changes, high gamma-ray flux, and correlated emission regions.

quotes.quote: In this work, I have presented a multi-frequency variability and correlation study of the blazar Ton 599, which was observed first time in flaring state at the end of 2017. Data from Fermi LAT, Swift-XRT/UVOT, Steward Observatory, and OVRO (15 GHz) is used, and it is found that the source is more variable in ray and optical/UV than X-ray and radio. Large variations in the degree of polarization (DoP) and position angle (PA) is noticed during the flaring period. Maximum flux during ray flare is found to be 12.63 10 ^ 7 at MJD 58057.5 from the 1-day bin light curve (LC), which is the maximum flux ever achieved by this source.

Ton 599 is a FSRQ also known as 4C 29.45, and 3FGL J1159.5+2914 ( Acero et al. (2015) ) with RA = 179.8826413 deg, Dec = 29.2455075 deg, and z = 0.72449. This is the first time the source has gone through a long flaring state across the entire electromagnetic spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper monitors blazar 4C + 29.45, derives radio variability, turnover frequencies, magnetic-field strengths, and concludes its synchrotron self-absorption region is particle dominated.

quotes.quote: We present the results of multi-epoch, multi-frequency monitoring of a blazar 4C  + 29.45, which was regularly monitored as part of the Interferometric Monitoring of GAmma-ray Bright Active Galactic Nuclei (iMOGABA) program - a key science program of the Korean Very long baseline interferometry Network (KVN). Observations were conducted simultaneously at 22, 43, 86, and 129 GHz during the 4 years from 5 December 2012 to 28 December 2016. We also used additional data from the 15 GHz Owens Valley Radio Observatory (OVRO) monitoring program.

4C  + 29.45 (also known as 1156+295, J1159+2914, and TON 599) is classified as a blazar  ver+10 with a redshift of z=0.729   zhao+11,wang+14 . It shows a wide time scale range of variations from hours to years  liu+13,wang+14, aha+07,rai+13 . Many previous studies have attempted to explain these variations  liu+13,wang+14, aha+07,rai+13 .

sources.summary: The paper reports QSO 1156+295 as a z=0.729 quasar, alias 4C +29.45, with unusually rapid, high-amplitude 15 GHz IDV and a core-jet, core-dominated VLBI morphology.

quotes.quote: We report here the serendipitous discovery of one new source, QSO 1156+295, showing IDV on a timescale of less than 3 h and with 13 rms amplitude of modulation. This discovery is unusual in two ways. Firstly, it was made during a VLBI experiment, and secondly, the modulation index of 13 is atypically high at 15 GHz. 1156+295 (4C +29.45) is a quasar at z=0.729 that is strongly variable throughout its spectrum from radio to gamma-rays . It has a core-jet morphology in VLBI maps and it appeared strongly core-dominated in 2007 (Fig. map ).

sources.summary: The paper says 1156+295 has weak jet emission that prevents core-shift measurements, but aligned maps show a strongly inverted low-frequency core that flattens at high frequencies.

quotes.quote: For these two sources, we were unable to obtain core-shift measurements due to the weak nature of the jet emission. Hence, in this case we cannot be sure if the core corresponds to a stationary feature or the =1 surface. To produce the spectral index maps (Figs. 1156spix , 1749spix ), we aligned the images by their model-fitted core positions. This produced uniform flat spectra in the core regions (e.g., the slice along the 4.6--7.9 GHz spectral index map for 1156+295, Fig. 1156spix a). We can see that the core spectrum of 1156+295, is strongly inverted ( _ 7.9 GHz ^ 4.6 GHz 1.3 ) at low frequencies and then flattens considerably at the higher frequencies with _ 43 GHz ^ 22 GHz 0.05 (Fig. 1156spix ).

sources.summary: The paper reports J1159 + 2914 as an FSRQ with confirmed INOV on all six Noble monitoring nights and large internight brightness changes.

quotes.quote: J1159 + 2914 (4C 29.45; z = 0.729 ) Confirmed INOV was detected on all the 6 nights this FSRQ was monitored by Noble (1995), with ranging between 4 and 12 per cent (Table 3). It has also shown large variations on internight time scales. Firstly, it brightened by 6 per cent between 19 and 21 Jan. \ 1994 and then faded by 0.5 mag between 21 and 22 Jan.1994. It again brightened by 7 per cent between 22 and 23 Jan. \ 1994 and was last found having faded by 0.3 mag on the following night.

sources.summary: The paper says 1156+295 was previously monitored and studied with VLBI, then finds its flux density doubled linearly over seven months while retaining an inverted spectrum.

quotes.quote: 1156+295 This source has also been included in monitoring campaigns, e.g. , and studied with dedicated VLBI observations Zhao_et_al_2011 . As evident from Fig. fig:fm_1156+295 , the flux density of 1156+295 increases by a factor 2 in a linear fashion during the 7 months spanned by our monitoring. The four bands follow each other, keeping the inverted (more power at higher frequencies) spectrum from start to end. We do not detect significant variability in our short IDVM sessions, see Fig. fig:idvm_1156+295 .

sources.summary: The paper highlights FSRQ 1156+295 as showing a possible opposite spectral-slope trend between faint and bright states, supporting a mix of thermal and nonthermal variability behavior.

quotes.quote: For the FSRQ 1156+295, there is a hint that the slope gets flatter at both the brightest and faintest states.
It is interesting that the tendency is possibly opposite in the faint state against the bright state for 1156+295.

The variability of 1156+295 is a good support to brown1989a,brown1989b , they mentioned that in high brightness state the spectra variability of FSRQs showed similar properties with that of BL Lacs. But in the faint state, since the thermal contribution was larger in the blue part, the nonthermal component was steeper than the composite spectrum.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1156+295 as an extremely variable quasar with a straight arcsecond jet and curved milliarcsecond jet; observations found a major March 2006 optical outburst and a concurrent increasing radio trend.

quotes.quote: 1156+295 This quasar is extremely variable over all the electromagnetic spectrum, from radio waves to rays 1983ApJ...274...62W,1992ApJ...398..454W,1999ApJS..123...79H . 1156+295 has a straight arcsecond scale jet and a more curved jet on milliarcsecond scales, with jet components moving at superluminal speeds of up to 16 (e.g. \ 2004A&A...417..887H ). Our observations reveal a major optical outburst in March 2006 (Fig. fig:olc ). An increasing trend is also visible in the radio light (Fig. fig:lc ) and it will be interesting to see if we can find a connection between the two events.

sources.summary: The paper treats 1156+295 as a blazar whose rotation-measure profile changes from nearly linear to dramatically bent and then roughly linear across frequency bands.

quotes.quote: OSul-Gabu:09 present profiles for six blazars, as measured in a variety of frequency bands. In at least two cases the profiles display the bent shape characteristic of helical motion: 1156+295 and 1418+546. In 1418+546 this is seen clearly in a profile constructed from observations between 4.6 and 8.9 . At higher frequencies, the profile shows evidence of being reversed, though the absolute is reduced by nearly two orders of magnitude, again consistent with the presence of relativistic motion. In contrast, at 4.6 - 8.9 , 1156+295 exhibits a nearly linear profile, while at 7.9 - 15.4 , the profile becomes dramatically bent. At higher frequencies ( 12.9 - 43 ), it becomes roughly linear again, which may be evidence of reduced u^z at small radii, a particularly fortuitous view of the transition to a reversed profile, or failure to fully resolve the gradient.

sources.summary: The paper models 1156+295 as a flaring FSRQ, using its orphan and non-orphan flares to probe shock interactions that shape flare properties.

quotes.quote: The selected sources were the QSO 0420-014, the BL Lac object OJ 287, and the FSRQ 1156+295.

Section orphan explores how changing the number and strength of shocks contributing to a single radio outburst impacts the light curve in the context of the `orphan flare' in the light curve of 1156+295.

While the spatial and temporal resolution of VLBI data are not sufficient to convincingly address the possibility of shock interactions (and if these are occurring upstream of the 43 GHz core only higher frequency data could probe such dynamics), our result for the orphan and non-orphan flares in the source 1156+295 (Section orphan ) provides evidence that interactions do occur, and play an important role in establishing the flare properties.

sources.summary: The paper confirms a previously reported 1156+295 core-region RM gradient with 5.8 sigma significance and groups it among robust monotonic core gradients.

quotes.quote: 1156+295. An RM map made from the same visibility data was published by Gabuzda et al. (2008). Our analysis shows that the previously reported monotonic, transverse RM gradient across the core region has a significance of 5.8 .
Monotonic transverse RM gradients with significances of at least 3 are observed across the core regions of 0355+508, 0748+126, 1156+295, 1749+096, 2007+777 and 2155 152.

gmw_00000433.name: 1ES 1218+304
gmw_00000433.overview: 1ES 1218+304 is a high-frequency-peaked BL Lacertae object [arXiv:1908.11836, arXiv:2006.16030] located at a redshift of z = 0.182 [arXiv:1205.2399, arXiv:1908.11836]. The active galactic nucleus features a radio jet extending to the east [arXiv:1205.2399] and exhibits multiwavelength variability, including gamma-ray and X-ray flaring episodes [arXiv:1001.2590, arXiv:1908.11836].

sources.summary: The paper uses non-detection of angularly broadened emission from 1ES 1218+304 to constrain cascade fractions and exclude a range of intergalactic magnetic-field strengths.

quotes.quote: Through non-detection of angularly broadened emission from 1ES 1218+304, the source with the largest predicted cascade fraction, we exclude a range of IGMF strengths around 10^ 14 G at the 95 confidence level. The extent of the exclusion range varies with the assumptions made about the intrinsic spectrum of 1ES 1218+304 and the EBL model used in the simulation of the cascade process.

For the assumptions about the intrinsic spectrum and EBL intensity taken, the predicted cascade fraction is below the VERITAS sensitivity for all of the sources other than 1ES 1218+304. However, these assumptions are subject to large uncertainties which strongly affect the predicted cascade fraction, as will be shown in Section IGMFconstraints . Consequently, although only 1ES 1218+304 will be used to set limits on the cascade fraction and IGMF strength, the other sources are included in the analysis, in light of the possibility that the cascade fraction could be underpredicted for the nominal set of assumptions.

For 1ES 1218+304, the predicted f_ is 10-25 for the range of magnetic fields considered, as shown in Fig. casfrac_pred_UL . Consequently, of all the sources studied, only 1ES 1218+304 is used to place constraints on the IGMF strength. While a stacked analysis of all sources at similar redshifts was feasible, the combined limit would be entirely dominated by the contributions of 1ES 1218+304.

sources.summary: The paper presents a 2018-2019 gamma-ray campaign on 1ES 1218+304, describing its BL Lac nature, previous VHE detections, active multiwavelength state, and VERITAS monitoring.

quotes.quote: is a high-frequency-peaked BL Lac Object located at a redshift of z = 0.182. It was discovered as a VHE emitter by the MAGIC collaboration during an observation campaign in 2005. Between the nights of 25th January 2009 and 5th February 2009, day-scale variability was observed with the flux reaching a peak of 20 Crab on the night of 30th January 2009.

During late 2018, entered an active state across multiple wavelengths, with enhanced activity at both X-ray https://swift.gsfc.nasa.gov/results/transients/weak/QSOB1218p304/ https://www.swift.psu.edu/monitoring/source.php?source=1ES1218+304 and optical energies Tuorla - http://users.utu.fi/kani/1m/1ES 1218+304.html . During a regularly scheduled monitoring observation on 3rd January 2019, VERITAS observed an enhanced TeV level of > 20 from . VERITAS continued to monitor at a high-cadence until 3rd April 2019. During this period a historic peak in the X-ray count rate was observed by . In these proceedings, the gamma-ray data taken by VERITAS and from the 2018-2019 season are presented.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES1218+30.4 as an HBL, adjusts minimum electron energy for a better SED fit, and derives an EBL-density range from BPL and PLLP fits.

quotes.quote: 1. 1ES1218+30.4 . As an HBL, we take t_ v,min =12 hr from the light curve acc2010 . used '_ min =300. However, in our paper, we set '_ min =100 to get a better fitting result. From Fig. 1, we can see that the SED is well fitted by BPL and PLLP EEDs.

The reduced EBL density shown in the right panel of Fig.8 is lower than that obtained by galaxy counts in the BPL EEDs, but it is roughly consistent with Stecker06-baseline ste2006 in the PLLP EEDs shown in the left panel of Fig.8. It is noted that the lower errors are not plotted for their larger uncertain. Besides, the 1 contour shows that the distribution of a_0 and E_0 are not constrained very well. The proper EBL density obtained by this source could be between 1.5 and 11 n W m ^ 2 sr ^ 1 .

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1218-304 among six extreme BL Lacs, explaining its broadband SED with a two-zone lepto-hadronic scenario involving inner-blob photohadronic and outer-blob SSC/EC emission.

quotes.quote: Finally, using the parameters listed in Table tab_model_parameters we model successfully the broadband SED of six known extreme BL Lac 1ES 0229+200, RGB J0710+591, 1ES 0347-121, 1ES 1101-232, 1ES 1218-304, 1ES 0414+009. The result of our model is shown in Figure ( fig_SED_result ). It is important to mention that in this work, we have not included the contribution observed in the optical band produced by the host galaxy Costamante2018 . The extreme BL Lac behavior presents challenges for a pure leptonic or hadronic model with one-zone emission. These objects demand atypical parameters for explaining the hard spectrum shape, especially in a VHE gamma-ray band.

In particular cases, we described the six best-known extreme BL Lacs using our proposed model, and we found a feasible explanation that relaxes the atypical values required by the one-zone models. We showed that the annihilation line radiation produced in a mildly relativistic outflow pair-plasma and the DT play essential roles in forming the hard spectrum observed in the VHE gamma-ray bands. In our model, this gamma-ray bump is produced mainly in two regions: i) the inner blob, which is responsible for the highest energy contribution above TeV-energies via photo-hadronic interactions with the annihilation line photons from the pair-plasma, and ii) the outer blob, where the very-high-energy gamma rays are created by EC with seed photons from the DT, and by the SSC mechanism.

sources.summary: The paper uses 1ES 1218+304 as a BL Lac source with VERITAS-dominated TeV data, measured redshift, HAWC optical depth, modest flux increase, and SSC emission-region parameters.

quotes.quote: The BL Lac object 1ES 1218 has been observed by VERITAS and MAGIC for 27.2 and 8.2 hrs, respectively. Note that observations by MAGIC were also performed in 2010 and 2011 during an observation campaign for 1ES 1215+303, where the source always stayed in the field-of-view. However, no energy spectrum is publicly available from this observation campaign . Observations by VERITAS extend to 1.8 TeV while the observations from MAGIC extend to 0.6 TeV. This corresponds to an optical depth of 2.1 and 1.1 respectively, with a redshift of z = 0.182 .

The source has been observed in the GeV range by the Fermi-LAT, with 4FGL observations reaching a significance of 69 and a variability index of 60. The maximum annual flux from the source (reached in mid-2018) is roughly 1.4 times brighter than the average source flux. Singh et al. found that the zone responsible for emission of VHE photons can be described by a one-zone SSC model with the following parameters: B = 0.22 G, R = 2.2 10^ 15 cm , = 26 and a viewing angle of 1.4^o .

sources.summary: The paper describes PG1218+304 as an HSP BL Lac and VHE source; uGMRT imaging finds it compact on kpc scales with a polarized steep-spectrum core.

quotes.quote: PG1218+304 After its discovery as an unidentified X-ray source in the 2A catalog Cooke1978 , this source was identified as a BL Lac object using radio and optical observations in 1979 Wilson1979 . It has been extensively monitored at optical frequencies and has been found to be highly variable Jannuzi1993 . It is an HSP blazar Lister2018,Ajello2022 . The first VHE ray signal from PG1218+304 with a 6.4 significance above an energy threshold of 0.12 TeV was discovered by the MAGIC telescope in 2005 Albert2006 .

We have found this source to be compact on kpc scales consistent with previous observations e.g. and references within Perlman1994, Laurent1993 . Our uGMRT 650 MHz image (Figure fig4:PG1218 ) also shows a polarized compact core that has a steep spectrum. The inferred B-field is parallel (poloidal) to the VLBI jet axis.

sources.summary: MAGIC detected 1ES 1218+304 after earlier upper limits, identifying it as MAGIC’s first discovered source and measuring a non-variable power-law VHE spectrum.

quotes.quote: The AGN 1ES 1218+304 was observed several times with the Whipple telescope between 1995 and 2000. The observations resulted in an upper flux limit above 350 GeV of 8.3 10^ 12 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 corresponding to 8 of the Crab Nebula flux . The source was also observed by HEGRA between 1996 and 2002 and an upper flux limit above 840 GeV of 2.67 10^ 12 ph cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 (or 12 of the Crab Nebula flux) was reported . MAGIC observed 1ES 1218+304 in seven nights in January 2005 (Table tab:1 ).

The observed excess of 560 events has a statistical significance of 6.4 standard deviations above 140 GeV. 1ES 1218+304 is the first source discovered by MAGIC. The nightly averaged ray lightcurve did not show signs of significant variability. The energy spectrum was fitted with a pure power law with a photon index =3.0 0.4 , and the determined flux level is below the upper limits at higher energies determined in the past.

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1218+304 as a high-peaking BL Lac at z=0.182, already detected at VHE, variable in VERITAS data, hard-spectrum, and useful for EBL or magnetic-field studies.

quotes.quote: 1ES 1218+304 is another high-peaking BL Lac object located only 0.8^ away from 1ES 1215+303. It has a redshift of 0.182 and was first detected to emit VHE rays by MAGIC in 2005 . In 2009 VERITAS reported fast variability from the source, the peak flux reaching 20 of the Crab Nebula flux . In the Fermi -LAT one year catalog the source is flagged non-variable. Since the measured VHE spectrum of 1ES 1218+304 is particularly hard for its redshift, an intrinsic SED with an inverse Compton peak above 1 TeV is expected.

The ray emission is thus strongly interacting with the extragalactic backgroung light (EBL) before reaching us, making the source a good candidate to probe the EBL or the extragalactic magnetic field .
As shown, 1ES 1218+304 is detected at a 12 significance level in both epochs, while 1ES 1215+303 is detected only in the 2011 data.

sources.summary: The paper models 1ES 1218+304 as a hard-TeV UHBL distinguished by day-scale VHE flaring, using a one-day variability time-scale in the hadronic parameter scan.

quotes.quote: Out of the 44 HBLs detected so far in the TeV range, five sources --- 1ES 0229+200, 1ES 0347-121, RGB J0710+591, 1ES 1101-232 and 1ES 1218+304 --- are characterized by particularly hard spectra with the high-energy peak above 1 TeV, which is consistent with their very hard and weak signal in the Fermi LAT band. Another common characteristic is the lack of observable variability on short time-scales of the very-high-energy flux with the exception of 1ES 1218+304, which showed VHE flaring activity on the scale of days, 1218_Veritas . Although the BL Lac object H 1426+428 belongs to this sub-class as well, since it was detected at VHE only during flaring activity without extensive multi-wavelength coverage 1426_hegra, Arache, 1426_whipple2, 1426_whipple, Leonardo09, wystan we decided to exclude it from our sample. The modelling of these ultra-high-frequency peaked BL Lacs UHBLs, also known as extreme BL Lacs, EHBLs, see e.g. for a more recent review Costamante01, Gunes13 with one-zone SSC scenarios, which usually yield good results for HBLs, is proving difficult in most cases, as can be seen from Table tab:uhbl , where some recent attempts at SSC modeling of the sources are summarized. A good representation of the complete SEDs requires either extremely high values of the minimum Lorentz factor of the electron distribution (1ES 1101-232, 1ES 0347-121), or an elevated Doppler factor (1ES 1218+304), or both (1ES 0229+200, RGB J0710+591).

For the five sources, we find two distinct sets of solutions, corresponding to the proton-synchrotron scenario and a lepto-hadronic scenario, dominated by emission from secondary particles from p- interactions. In the latter case, the SSC contribution is at a level comparable to the emission from hadronic processes, and justifies the '' lepto-hadronic '' classification.

The maximum value of R is determined from equation equation24 by assuming _ var =10 days, except for the modelling of 1ES 1218+304, for which a variability time-scale of one day has been adopted, based on observational evidence 1218_Veritas .

sources.summary: The paper describes 1ES 1218+304 as a hard-spectrum HBL useful for EBL and IGMF studies, with a strong 2019 VERITAS flare and contemporaneous X-ray peak.
