We find that the effects of baryonic physics modify the original CDM halo densities in such a way that the resulting profile is more compatible with the DM density of NGC 3198 derived using our new method. However, at large distances, 25 kpc, also this modified baryon CDM halo profile appears to create a tension with the derived DM halo density.

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 3198 has intrinsically obscured nuclear X-ray emission consistent with an obscured AGN and detects a radio point source at the optical nucleus.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 is located at a distance of 13.8 Mpc 2001ApJ...553...47F . Its spectrum is modeled with an absorbed power law, with the column density as a free parameter. The fit reveals a column density that is approximately 55 times higher than what is expected from Galactic absorption and can only be attributed to intrinsic obscuration.

This finding is in agreement with previous classification of NGC 3198 as an obscured AGN 2011ApJ...731...60G . The 0.5-10 keV source unabsorbed luminosity is 6.27 10^ 38 erg s ^ 1 . A radio point source (beam size of 0.28 0.26 at 7.45 GHz) is detected in both frequency bands.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3198 as a key outer-disk case, discussing its deep H I observations, photoionization explanation, azimuthal truncation, and unusually extended gas disk.

quotes.quote: In an important unpublished experiment (see van Gorkom 1991), van Albada et al observed the isolated spiral galaxy NGC 3198 for 100 hours with the VLA in order to reach a column density sensitivity of 4 10^ 18 cm ^ 2 , with the expectation of probing the \ emission to beyond R 60 kpc. To their surprise They would have been less surprised if the prescient paper of Bochkarev Sunyaev (1977) had not been unjustly overlooked. , the 5 10^ 19 cm ^ 2 and 4 10^ 18 cm ^ 2 contours were coincident within their 1' beam (2.7 kpc for an assumed distance of 9.4 Mpc, but see below).

Following earlier discussions of the influence of the CIB on neutral hydrogen in galaxy disks (Sunyaev 1969; Felten Bergeron 1969; Bochkarev Sunyaev 1977), Maloney (1993) showed that there should be a critical column density N_c in \ disks at which the gas would go from largely neutral to largely ionized, that N_c would not depend strongly on either the galaxy parameters or the intensity of the ionizing background, and that this transition could plausibly explain the \ truncation observed in NGC 3198, provided the CIB is of order _i 10^4 phot cm ^ 2 s ^ 1 .

Although reportedly the truncation in NGC 3198 is independent of azimuth (ruling out a warp, for example, as an explanation), only the major axis cut was available for analysis. There are noticeable differences in \ extent between the NE and SW sides of the disk, with the SW being more extended. However, such asymmetries are not really surprising due to the long dynamical timescales at these radii: the orbital period for gas at 30 kpc in NGC 3198 is more than 10^9 years.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3198 as a reference HALOGAS system, fitting a thick extraplanar HI layer with rotational lag, modest mass fraction, and little global inflow/outflow.

quotes.quote: The EPG of NGC 3198 To illustrate our method, we first treat in detail a single, reference system: NGC 3198. This galaxy was already studied by G13 using the same HALOGAS data as in the current study. G13 experimented with different models and concluded that the \ emission from this galaxy could not be reproduced by a single disc component, but it required the contribution of a thick ( 3 scale-height) layer of EPG featuring a vertical rotational lag of 15 in the approaching side and 7 in the receding side. The EPG, which was assumed to have the same surface density profile of the whole gas distribution, would account for 15 of the total \ content.

We find a value of 9.2 1.4 for the rotational lag, a precise estimate that falls well within the range quoted by G13 for the two sides of the galaxy, while the magnitude of the vertical and radial velocities are compatible with zero within their uncertainty. As for the mass fraction of the EPG ( f_ EPG ), derived as the ratio between the \ flux in the unmasked EPG model and the total flux in the unmasked data cube, we find a value of 8.6 , in slight tension with respect to the 10-20 quoted in G13.

sources.summary: The paper interprets bumps in NGC 3198’s rotation curve as possible dark-matter caustic rings and compares its inferred halo angular momentum with the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: Consider the rotation curve of NGC 3198 , one of the best measured and often cited as providing compelling evidence for the existence of dark halos. It appears to have bumps near 28, 13.5 and 9 kpc, assuming h = 0.75 . Although the statistical significance of these bumps is not great, let's assume for the moment that they are real effects. Note then that their existence is inconsistent with the assumption that the dark halo is a perfect isothermal sphere.

On the other hand, the radii at which the bumps occur are in close agreement with the ratios: a_n/a_ n-1 : n=2,3,4,5 = (0.49, 0.67, 0.76, 0.81) predicted by Eq. (4) assuming that the bumps are caused by the gravitational fields of the first three caustic rings of NGC 3198. Since v_ rot = 150 km/sec, we find that j_ max = 0.28 in this case if = 0.3 .

sources.summary: The paper derives center estimates and harmonic-decomposition results for NGC 3198, describing its disk morphology, non-circular motions, and whether the inferred potential is consistent with being round.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 NGC 3198 (Figs. fig:n3198-1 fig:n3198-4 ) a) Center estimates [ 1.5ex ] NGC \ 3198 is classified as a SBc spiral. Its 3.6 m IRAC image shows two well-defined spiral arms, emanating from a prominent bulge. The central component has a nuclear point source embedded, which has a counterpart in the radio continuum. The center estimates from the IRAC image and the continuum map agree to within 1 .

sources.summary: The paper places this THINGS galaxy in Group B, where BDM fits outperform NFW, imply r_c roughly comparable to r_s, and favor a strong dark-matter core.

quotes.quote: In the second case, Group B, (IC 2574 (B1), NGC 2366 (B2), NGC 2903 (B3), NGC 2976 (B4), NGC 3198 (B5), NGC 3521 (B6), and NGC 925 (B7)), we get a fitted value of r_c r_s and BDM profile fits better than NFW. In fact, the NFW profile in some cases over-predicts the velocity in the inner parts of the galaxy or does fit with unphysical values for r_s > O (10^4) or _0 < O (10^3) M_ /kpc^ 3 , see Table tab:onlydm_bdm and tab:onlydm_nfw . The BDM profile gives r_c r_s for all stellar mass models, having an average r_c value of 6 and 5 kpc for Min.Disk and Min.Disk+Gas, respectively. Since r_c r_s , implying that our BDM profile is of the type (r_c + )^ 3 and hence having a strong core behavior. For this set of galaxies the Burkert and ISO profiles, as well as the BDM, fit much better than NFW, indicating that a core is needed.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 3198 using alternate stellar decompositions and THINGS or hybrid HERACLES/THINGS rotation curves, noting CO-HI shape differences and a high dust-to-gas conversion factor.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 In Figures fig:N3198-NFW and fig:N3198-ISO we plot the results of the mass modelling for NGC 3198. For NGC 3198 we use 1- and 2-component decompositions of the stellar surface brightness distribution to derive the predicted stellar rotation curves, and we treat the 1- and 2-component cases separately. In the analysis of the observed rotation curves for NGC 3198 presented in the Appendix we note a considerable difference in the shapes between the H i and CO observed rotation curves within 4 . We therefore consider two observed rotation curves for NGC 3198 - the THINGS rotation curve and a hybrid HERACLES/THINGS rotation curve, where we use the HERACLES rotation curve inside 4 and the THINGS rotation curve outside this radius. Therefore, for both the NFW and ISO models we have four scenarios, corresponding to the two possible stellar decompositions and the two observed rotation curves.

sources.summary: The paper models NGC 3198 rotation and acceleration curves, arguing its flat rotation curve can be fit without dark matter using multiple graviton interactions.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 The spiral disk galaxy NGC 3198 is shown in Fig. fig:3198jpg . Beg87 observed a large discrepancy between the actual RC and that predicted from the light distribution in this galaxy. KaSaGe state that it provides spectacular evidence of a dark force in action as baryons are unable to account for the kinematics. NGC 3198 has been studied by many groups e.g. Albetal,TaIy,BlAmCa,KadJoWei,Kos06,LovKie,KarSal,AlAmNi,DaoZek and we compiled some of the results in Figs. fig:3198jpg to fig:Blok as well as in Table tab:masses . We did, however, not refer to the many statements related to DM as we feel that our multiple interaction model can explain the flat RCs without a DM component.

sources.summary: For NGC 3198, MOND favors a distance about 10 percent below the Cepheid value; near-infrared photometry does not remove the rotation-curve discrepancy.

quotes.quote: For NGC 3198, the largest distance for which reasonable agreement is obtained is 10 smaller than the Cepheid-based distance; i.e., MOND clearly prefers a smaller distance. This conclusion is unaltered when new near-infrared photometry of NGC 3198 is taken as the tracer of the stellar mass distribution.

For NGC 3198 at the Cepheid-based distance of 13.8 Mpc, the MOND curve shows small ( <10 ) but significant systematic deviations from the observed curve. If the distance to NGC 3198 is taken to be 12.5 Mpc, or 10 less than the Cepheid-based distance, the MOND curve is an acceptable representation of the observed curve. This lower distance is probably within the uncertainties of the Cepheid method.

sources.summary: The paper fits NGC 3198 rotation data with Carmelian and Newtonian models, finding the Carmelian curve better matches the galaxy with lower inferred mass.

quotes.quote: Extragalactic spirals In this section, I discuss individual galaxy curve fits, starting with fig fig:fig2 showing the barbed spiral NGC 3198. In each case, because of the possibility of different acceleration regimes, both Carmelian and Newtonian curve fit were attempted. In fig fig:fig2 (a) the Carmelian fit is shown by the broken curve 2 to be the only good fit. The scale radius a = 1.85 kpc and M=0.984 10^ 10 M_ determined from the fit. Curve 3 shows the best Newtonian fit with a = 2.99 kpc and M=4.2 10^ 10 M_ but it doesn't fit well at high values of . The Newtonian fit results in a mass at least 4 times greater than that from the Carmelian fit. The scale radius determined from luminous matter for this galaxy is a=2.5 kpc which is closer to the Carmelian curve determination.

sources.summary: The paper says NGC 3198 has a poor MOND fit without the EFE, with outer velocities too high, but the EFE reduces chi-squared to 1.3.

quotes.quote: As shown in Figure 2 and Table 1, MOND without the EFE has a poor quality fit for NGC 3198 with a ^2 value of 5.49. This is because the MOND curve predicts a higher rotational velocity in the outer parts, which implies more mass, while it underestimates it in the inner part (between 3 and 15 kpc). Applying the EFE, our results show an excellent improvement in MOND fits by reducing the ^2 value to 1.3, with a required external field of about a_e=0.24 0.05 a_0 . This amount of external gravitational field is compatible with the possible external source that we have found in the literature.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes NGC 3198 as a barred spiral with nearly constant disk geometry and a very quiet velocity field showing bar-aligned coherent fluctuations.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 NGC 3198 is a barred spiral galaxy. According to the TRM, the inclination angle is close to constant at all radii and the P.A. also shows very little variation across the galactic disk, both inside and outside the optical radius is R_ 25 220'' (see Fig. NGC3198 ). Because of these small variations, we find that v_t(R) v_c(R) at all radii.

sources.summary: NGC 3198 is treated as a well-studied barred spiral in Ursa Major, with the paper comparing MOND-RAR behavior at Cepheid distance and its RCFM fit.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 NGC 3198 is a barred spiral in Ursa Major, which has been well studied. It has been considered a problem galaxy for MOND, when the distance is a free parameter in fits Gent , as the preferred MOND distance is 2 different from that reported from Cepheids. However, in the SPARC database the MOND-RAR fit to this galaxy at the Cepheid distance reproduce the RC well. See Fig.  fig1super for the RCFM fit.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3198 rotation-curve decomposition to argue for a submaximal disk, lowering the disk mass-to-light ratio and making the halo dominant inside the optical radius.

quotes.quote: Only in the cases of NGC 3198 and 6503 HI data were available, which allowed the determination of the v_ ,g contribution in (1).

This is illustrated in Fig. 5 for NGC 3198, where the mass-to-light ratio of the disk has been reduced from M/L_ B = 3.5 to 2.2 M_ /L_ B , . Within the optical radius the dark halo contributes twice the mass of the disk and its core radius is of the order of the radial scale length of the disk.
As can be seen in Fig. 5 and similarly in Fig. 6 for NGC 6503, the Q parameters lie in a more realistic range.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC3198 as an example of possible universal halo structure, interpreting three rotation-curve bumps as the first three predicted caustic rings.

quotes.quote: As a possible example of this effect, consider the rotation curve of NGC3198, one of the best measured and a member of the set selected in refs. . It has three faint bumps at radii: 28, 13.5 and 9 kpc, assuming h=0.75 . The ratios happen to be consistent with Eq.(1) assuming the bumps are caused by the first three ( n=1,2,3 ) ring caustics of NGC3198. Moreover, since v_ rot = 150 km/s, j_ max is determined in terms of .

sources.summary: The paper applies a rotation-curve residual power-spectrum method to NGC3198, finding evidence for large-scale turbulence and concluding the regular spiral likely experienced a minor merger about 7 Gyr ago.

quotes.quote: The method is applied to the regular spiral galaxy NGC3198 with the conclusion that it underwent a minor merger about 7 Gyr ago.
The method is demonstrated for the rotation curve of the spiral galaxy NGC3198 and leads to the conclusion that in spite its ordered shape, this galaxy has experienced a minor merger about 7 Gyr ago.
But even so, the case for an underlying 3D turbulence in NGC3198 seems quite good.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3198 as a bright high-surface-brightness rotation-curve example, discussing its inner Newtonian rise and conformal-gravity fit crossover behavior.

quotes.quote: We should also note that for bright spirals the conformal gravity fitting leads to a luminous Newtonian expectation that dominates the sharp initial rise of the inner region rotation curve (see e.g. the NGC 3198 plot that we provide in Fig. ( gangofthree ). This rise is characteristic of the exponential disk formula given in ( A2 ), and this natural explanation of the inner region rise would be lost with smaller M/L values, to then require some new dynamics in the inner regions of bright spirals.

As can be seen in the fits to UGC 128 and NGC 3198, in conformal gravity fits to rotation curves the conformal gravity contribution and the Newtonian contribution typically cross in a region far enough out from the center of the galaxy so that the galaxy can be treated as a point source, and close enough in that the quadratic term in ( A4 ) is negligible.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 3198 as a very inclined galaxy, applies azimuthal normalization for low-S/N data, and finds its HI inclination effect less prominent than NGC 2841.

quotes.quote: Such effects may be present in two further profiles of very inclined galaxies, NGC 2841 and NGC 3198, but they seem to be less affected.
For this reason, when the S/N in the 400 pc resolution data cube is low (as for the most distant galaxies, NGC 2841, NGC 3198, and NGC 7331), we choose the azimuthal normalisation.
The effect of inclination on _ HI profile of NGC 3198 is less prominent with respect to NGC 2841, as the former galaxy is less inclined.

sources.summary: The paper includes NGC 3198 despite its high inclination, uses HALOGAS data, and reports less radially extended star formation than H I.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 instead, despite the relatively high inclination ( i 72 ), appears much less affected by this issue and is then included in our sample (see Appendix D in 2019Bacchini for a more detailed discussion).

For NGC 0925, NGC 2403, NGC 3198, and NGC 5055, we used the publicly available data cubes from the Hydrogen Accretion in LOcal GAlaxieS (HALOGAS) Survey 2011Heald , which have a better signal-to-noise ratio with respect to the robust-weighted THINGS data cubes adopted in 2019Bacchini .
For DDO 154, NGC 2403, NGC 3198, and NGC 6946, _ SFR (R) is less radially extended than _ HI (R) , as the FUV emission from the outermost radii goes below the sensitivity limit of the observations.

sources.summary: The paper reports that NGC 3198 is very well fit by a flat axisymmetric model, with rotation curve and uncertainties matching previous estimates.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198 A flat axisymmetric model fits the data from this galaxy very well, as is evident from Figure  fig.N3198 . Our fitted rotation curve and uncertainties are in good agreement with those estimated by dB08. SZS10 , who used an earlier version of \ to search for non-axisymmetic distortions, failed to find any evidence for a distortion in the mid-plane potential of this well-studied galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 3198 to show IR metallicity can exceed optical estimates, attributing the difference to obscuration from a bright IR nucleus hidden in UV and optical images.

quotes.quote: Still, this discrepancy can be important for individual galaxies, e.g. the IR metallicity in NGC 3198, 12 + (O/H)_ IR = 8.96 0.05 , is a factor of two larger when compared to the optical metallicity, 12 + (O/H)_ opt = 8.65 0.03 (see Fig fig_IRopt ). This is likely caused by obscuration, since NGC 3198 shows a bright IR nucleus which is not revealed in the UV and optical images gildepaz2007,brown2014,kennicutt2011 , while a relatively strong absorption is detected in the silicate band at 9.7 ( S_ sil = -1.9 ; smith2007 ).
We confirm that obscuration is one of the factors contributing to the scatter between the IR and optical abundances, shown by the case of NGC 3198.

sources.summary: The paper groups NGC 3198 with low-halo-mass outliers, saying it lies above the massive-galaxy relation and may not host a supermassive black hole.

quotes.quote: This tendency becomes more pronounced for halos with M_ DM 5 10^ 11 : as mentioned above, NGC 6503, NGC 3198 and NGC 598 lie above the relation defined by the more massive galaxies. It must be pointed out, however, that the relation is not defined for < 10^6 , indeed we have no direct evidence of the existence of nuclear black holes in this mass range. If such SBHs exist, the recent report of an upper limit of only a few 10^3 for the BH in NGC 598 (Merritt, Ferrarese Joseph 2001; Gebhardt et al. 2001), indicates that they are not more massive than would be inferred by extrapolating the relation to the _c 20 range. This excludes the possibility that NGC 6503 and NGC 3198 could be reconciled with the relation defined by the more massive galaxies.
It is therefore possible that galaxies such as NGC 6503 and NGC 3198 do not contain SBHs.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC 3198 as an SB(rs) galaxy with prior spectroscopic and Fabry-Perot study, central non-circular motions, and a strong velocity gradient perpendicular to the bar.

quotes.quote: NGC 3198: This SB(rs) galaxy has been extensively studied in \ (Bosma 1981a, 1989A&A...223...47B 1989A&A...223...47B ), FP ( 1991A&A...244...27C 1991A&A...244...27C , Blais-Ouellette et al. 1999) and and [ ] long-slit spectroscopy ( 1998PASJ...50..427S 1998PASJ...50..427S , 2004AJ....127.3273V 2004AJ....127.3273V ). According to the PV diagram, non circular motions near the centre can be seen. A strong velocity gradient is also seen perpendicular to the bar major axis.

sources.summary: The paper notes that NGC 3198 has a Cepheid distance, but its best-fit MOND distance is about two times smaller and forced Cepheid fits worsen.

quotes.quote: Note however that three galaxies (NGC 2403, NGC 3198, and NGC 7331) also have Cepheid-based distances . While for NGC 2403 and NGC 7331 the best-fit distances (of both functions) are within 10 of the Cepheid distances, the best-fit distance of NGC 3198 is about a factor 2 smaller than the Cepheid-based one. Imposing this value of the distance slightly decreases the best-fit value of a_0 but it doesn't change the best-fit value of , nor does it change significantly the result of Eq. ( chi ), because the fits with both functions get worse for NGC 3198.

gmw_00000449.name: B0218+357
aliases_local: z=0.69 QAS toward the Quasar TXS 0218+357
gmw_00000449.overview: B0218+357 is a gravitationally lensed blazar [arXiv:1509.00012, arXiv:1401.0548] and flat-spectrum radio quasar [arXiv:2003.03841, arXiv:1609.01095] located at a redshift of approximately 0.94 [arXiv:1106.2471, arXiv:1609.01095]. The system features two compact, highly variable radio images [arXiv:1106.2471, arXiv:1601.03591] and an Einstein ring [arXiv:1106.2471, arXiv:0910.0800] lensed by a foreground spiral galaxy [arXiv:1106.2471, arXiv:2003.03841].

sources.summary: The paper describes B0218+357 as a LAT-detected gravitationally lensed blazar with an 11.46-day flare delay and possible microlensing changes constraining the gamma-ray emission region.

quotes.quote: Recently, the first two gravitationally lensed blazars have been detected by the LAT: PKS 1830-211 and B0218+357 . In these systems two (or more) images are obtained from one object in the optical and radio. Due to the limited angular resolution, these two images cannot be resolved by the LAT. However, due to the different travelling times around the lensing galaxy, a time delay of the signal can be detected.

For B0218+357, a time delay of 11.46 0.16 days was detected for the flaring activity of 2012 . In 2014, a new flare was detected, as shown in fig. fig:agnlensed . However, the expected delayed flare (if present) was much weaker than expected. This points toward changes in the micro lensing properties in the lensing galaxy.

sources.summary: The review describes QSO B0218+357 as a redshift 0.944 lensed blazar whose Fermi and MAGIC flares yielded a measured delay and the first VHE lensed-blazar detection.

quotes.quote: It was the case for QSO B0218+357, also known as S3 0218+35, a blazar located at the redshift of 0.944. QSO B0218+357 is one of only two objects with a measured gravitational lensing effect in GeV energy range. In 2012, it went through a series of outbursts registered by the Fermi LAT instrument. The statistical analysis of the light curve autocorrelation function led to a measurement of time delay between the direct and the lensed components of 11.46 0.16 days.

Another flaring state of QSO B0218+357 was observed by Fermi LAT in July 2014. Unfortunately, the first component was not visible by MAGIC due to the full moon period but they could catch the second (lensed) component. It is therefore the first gravitationally lensed blazar detected at the VHE energies. Interestingly, the emission is very bright despite the attenuation at such redshift which can be used to put constraints on the EBL.

sources.summary: The paper studies the lensed blazar JVAS B0218+357 with VLBA monitoring, finding compact lensed images, no significant radio variability after high-energy activity, resolved core-jet subcomponents, and no morphology change.

quotes.quote: B0218+357 was identified as a lensing system by 1992LNP...406..140P and is one of the brightest lensed objects in the Cosmic Lens All-Sky Survey (CLASS) sample ( 2003MNRAS.341...13B , 2003MNRAS.341....1M ). This radio source consists of two compact images (A and B) separated by 335 mas and an Einstein ring with a similar size ( 1992AJ....104.1320O , 2001MNRAS.322..821B ).

We have presented results on a multi-frequency VLBA monitoring campaign of the ray flaring gravitationally lensed blazar JVAS B0218+357. The observations were spread over a two-month period and started roughly one month after the ray flaring activity detected by Fermi LAT on 2012 August. Although the source was very active in the rays, it did not show any significant variability in the radio band, precluding us from investigating any possible connection between the high-energy and low-energy emission.

The high angular resolution of our VLBA data allowed us to resolve the source images into subcomponents and determining their core-jet structure. Some distortion effects are present in component A, where at 2.3 and 8.4 GHz the core-jet structure is hidden by diffuse emission. No evidence of a change in the source morphology was found by comparing the multi-epoch images at 22 GHz, supporting the steady behaviour of the source in the radio band.

sources.summary: QSO B0218+357 is presented as a z=0.944 blazar, the first gravitationally lensed blazar detected at very high energies, with MAGIC detecting delayed flare emission.

quotes.quote: The two sources detected and analyzed, the blazar QSO B0218+357 and the FSRQ PKS 1441+25 are located at redshift z=0.944 and z=0.939 respectively. QSO B0218+357 is also the first gravitational lensed blazar ever detected in VHE.

QSO B0218+357 QSO B0218+357 is a blazar located at the redshift of 0.944 . The object is gravitationally lensed by a galaxy [ PBK93 ] B0218+357 G located at z=0.68 . The time delay between the two flares, measured by Fermi LAT during a previous flare in 2012, was expected to be 11.46 0.16 days. In July 2014 a flare observed by Fermi LAT triggered follow-up observations of the MAGIC telescopes which in turn led to the discovery of VHE ray emission from QSO B0218+357 from the secondary component of the flare, observed at the expected time of arrival with a significance of 5.7 (see Fig. fig:th2 , panel a).

QSO B0218+357 ( z=0.944 ) and PKS 1441+25 ( z=0.939 ) were detected for the first time in the VHE range by MAGIC, extending the maximum redshift for VHE ray observations up to a distance z 1 , near the cosmic ray horizon. QSO B0218+357 was the first ever gravitationally lensed blazar detected in the VHE, and the analysis for this peculiar source is still ongoing .

sources.summary: The paper finds J0221.1+3556 may have power-law quiescent emission, softer emission during its flare, a complex two-peak 3-day light curve, and stable spectra before and after the flare.

quotes.quote: However from the \ spectra we obtained (see below Section sec:spe ), we noted that five sources (J0221.1+3556, J0236.8 6136, J0629.3 1959, J1617.9 7718, and J1748.6+7005), provided with spectral models of a log-parabola in 4FGL, might have power-law emission instead, particularly in the quiescent states. We re-ran the likelihood analysis assuming a power-law model for them in the source models, and found that a log-parabola is not significantly preferred over a power law in the quiescent states of the first four sources, while for J1748.6+7005, its emission in the flaring state can be described with a power law.

For the other 8 sources, five did not have significant spectral changes but three of them (J0221.1+3556, J1040.5+0617, and J1748.6+7005) had softer emission when in flares. Examining them, the 3-day binned light curve of J0221.1+3556 shows a complex flaring pattern with two major peaks, while the latter two had relatively ``active" quiescent states: J1040.5+0617 had as large as a factor of 3 flux variations and J1748.6+7005 showed minor activities following the flare, and returned to the lowest-flux level at the end of its long-term light curve.

We therefore chose three relatively bright sources: J0221.1+3556, J0510.0+1800, and J0958.7+6534, as they were detected in nearly all the 90-day bins. We checked their spectral properties in one year time durations before and after the flares respectively. The results during the two time periods are consistent within uncertainties; the sources did not have any significant changes in spectra. We thus conclude that the sources likely have relatively stable emission in quiescence before and after the flare.

sources.summary: QSO B0218+357/S3 0218+35 is a VHE-detected lensed FSRQ at z=0.944; 2016-2020 monitoring found optical, X-ray and GeV flares but no contemporaneous VHE or radio enhancement.

quotes.quote: QSO B0218+357 , also known as S3 0218+35, is one of only a handful of Flat Spectrum Radio Quasars (FSRQs) detected in very-high-energy (VHE, 100 GeV) gamma-ray emission. It has a redshift of z_s=0.944 0.002 co03, pa17 . The source showed strong variability in the GeV range in 2012 ch14 when a series of flares was observed by Fermi Large Area Telescope (LAT). Another flare was observed by \ in 2014, and during the follow-up the source was discovered in VHE gamma rays by MAGIC telescopes 2015arXiv150203134B, 2015ICRC...34..877B, ah16 .

We report multiwavelength observations of the gravitationally lensed blazar in 2016-2020. Optical, X-ray and GeV flares were detected. The contemporaneous MAGIC observations do not show significant very-high-energy (VHE, 100 GeV) gamma-ray emission. The lack of enhancement in radio emission measured by OVRO indicates the multi-zone nature of the emission from this object. We constrain the VHE duty cycle of the source to be <16 2014-like flares per year (95 confidence).

sources.summary: The paper searches for microlensing in lensed blazar B0218+357 and uses delayed gamma-ray images to constrain the compact VHE emission-region size.

quotes.quote: We search for the microlensing in the signal from a gravitationally lensed blazar B0218+357 in very-high-energy \ band. We combine observations of a bright flare of the source in 2014 with Fermi/LAT and MAGIC telescopes in 0.1-100 GeV energy range. Using the time-delayed leading and trailing signals from two gravitationally lensed images of the source, we measure magnification factor at the moment of the flare. We use the scaling of the maximal magnification factor with the source size to constrain the size of \ emission region in the entire 0.1-100 GeV energy range.

Here we report detection of the microlensing effect in B0218+357 in the VHE band, at the energy E_ 100 GeV. This source at redshift z=0.944 0.002 2000ApJ...545..578C has two images, A and B 1995MNRAS.274L...5P with the flux from the image B delayed by 11.4 0.2 d with respect to the image A 2018MNRAS.476.5393B . This allows to distinguish contributions of the images A and B to the \ flux even if the two images are not resolved in the \ band.
One can see that the size of the VHE emission region is found to be very compact with R_ VHE = 2.2^ +27 _ 1.7 10^ 13 cm .

sources.summary: JVAS0218+357 is described as a near-golden gravitational lens: radio structure breaks degeneracies, while HST reveals an M101-like spiral lensing galaxy.

quotes.quote: The system JVAS0218+357 is such a system. It is a radio double that, at VLBI resolution, has a core and a blob of the sort that radio astronomers fancifully call jets. The double source breaks the concentration degeneracy, and implies an isothermal potential. have modeled the radio data, finding the radial exponent and the center of symmetry of the lensing potential (see also Wucknitz' contribution to the present proceedings). The time delay has been measured with better than 5 accuracy .

The pairs of images are separated by only 0. ^ 33 , implying a low luminosity lens, almost certainly not an elliptical. have co-added data from a large number of HST orbits (see also Jackson and York's contribution to the present proceedings). After careful PSF subtraction of the two quasar images, their data clearly shows an M101-like spiral.

sources.summary: The paper treats JVAS B0218+357 as a double-image galactic-scale lens with a blazar source and spiral lens galaxy, arguing that free-free absorption can explain its frequency-dependent image-flux anomaly.

quotes.quote: The double-image gravitational lens was discovered in the Jodrell-VLA Very Large Array, NRAO Astrometric Survey (JVAS) of radio sources Patnaik1992b . It has the smallest angular image-separation ( 330 mas) amongst the known galactic-scale lens systems. The lensed object is a powerful radio galaxy (blazar) at a redshift of 0.944 Cohen2003 with a typical core-jet morphology and a frequency-dependent structure. Further, it has a variable radio emission and the time-delay between variations in the images has been accurately measured by Biggs1999 , (10.5 0.4) days , which is consistent with the value of (10.1 ^ +1.5 _ 1.6 ) days measured by Cohen2000 . The lens is at a redshift of 0.684 see also O'Dea et al. 1992 Browne1993 and is believed to be a spiral galaxy based on the small angular image-separation and radio absorption lines that have been measured for this system.

Following this, we analyze the effects of mechanisms which are non-gravitational in nature on the image flux ratios in . These are free-free absorption and scattering, and are assumed to occur under the hypothesis of a molecular cloud residing in the lens galaxy along the line-of-sight to image A. We show that free-free absorption due to an H ii region covering the entire structure of image A at 1.65 GHz can explain the image flux ratio anomaly.

sources.summary: The paper treats B0218+357 as a spiral lens system whose differential Faraday rotation implies a microgauss-scale line-of-sight magnetic field in the lensing galaxy.

quotes.quote: In our sample we have two lens systems, B0218+357 and PKS1830--211, where the lens is confirmed to be a spiral galaxy. There is a large rotation measure 8000 rad m ^ 2 that is common to both the images A, B in the lens system B0218+357. The large intrinsic RM could be from absorbing clouds en route.

However, the difference in RM between images B and A, which is found to be 980 10 rad m ^ 2 when the Faraday Rotation angle for four frequencies between 8.4 and 43 GHz in patn:93 is used. This could conceivably be the contribution of the lens galaxy. The result should be trustworthy considering the error estimate, since for an uncertainty in the Position Angle of polarization vector of 2^ , the ^2 is 0.65 for 2 degrees of freedom. With the neutral hydrogen column density of 2 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 and using the electron column density N_e 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 , we get from eq. (3), the mean field magnetic component, along the sightline, in the lensing galaxy of order 1 G .

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes JVAS B0218+357 light curves at 8.4 and 15 GHz, confirming the published roughly 10-day delay while noting the data cannot support 5 percent precision.

quotes.quote: JVAS B0218+357 We have used the data set published by 2000ApJ...545..578C , consisting of 51 flux density measurements at 8.4 GHz and 15 GHz. The authors obtained a time delay t_ AB = 10.1_ 1.6 ^ +1.5 days, where A is the leading image, thus confirming independently two values published earlier by 1999MNRAS.304..349B and 1996IAUS..173...37C of t_ AB = 10.5 0.4 days and t_ AB = 12 3 days, respectively.

Taking into account all points of the 15 GHz light curve provided a comparable value of the time delay of t_ AB = 11.1_ 1.1 ^ +4.0 , even though we noted that the importance of the secondary peak around t_ AB 14 days was significantly lower after we had eliminated outlying points in both the A and B curve, in the same way as for the 8.4 GHz curve. This suggests that the secondary peak around t_ AB 14 days is probably caused by artefacts in the data, hence we can confirm with confidence the previously published results: combining the values based on the 8.4 GHz and 15 GHz light curves gives a time delay of t_ AB = 9.9_ 0.9 ^ +4.0 days. The MD method confirms the strong influence of these deviating points: the secondary peak around t_ AB 14 days even completely disappears when they are removed from both the 8.4 GHz and the 15 GHz light curves. The 8.4 GHz curve gives a time delay of t_ AB = 12.6 2.9 days, and the 15 GHz data lead to t_ AB = 11.0 3.5 days, which gives a combined result of t_ AB = 11.8 2.3 days, all in agreement with the above-mentioned values. Even if all of these time delay values for this object are in agreement with each other, the data do not allow a precision of the order of 5 in the delays, which would be necessary for a useful estimate of H_ 0 .

sources.summary: The paper treats B0218+357 as a lensed jet source whose image positions, ring structure, and microarcsecond jet angle can constrain lens models and Hubble-constant estimates.

quotes.quote: The source B0218+357 ( z = 0.96   ) with a large-scale jet is a ''gold lens'' for the aforesaid goal because the time delay between the two compact core images has been measured with a good accuracy  , and there is no gravitational field distortion from nearby sources. But at the same time there is a significant uncertainty in determining the relative position of the lens (spiral galaxy) and the source  , which cannot be measure with present-day optic and infrared instruments due to a small angular separation of them. Therefore it is necessary to construct an accurate model of the system to evaluate the Hubble constant.

It was shown that there exists a fairly wide choice of model parameters adequately reproducing the large scale observed properties of B0218+357 (the intensity ratio of images A and B for the compact source I_A/I_B (3.1-3.7) , the image separation d 335 milliarcseconds (mas), the position angle of the large-scale jet) but leading to a significant spread in the Hubble constant  . Furthermore the ring-like structure (see Fig.1, left panel  ) observed in the radio band and produced by the lensing of the large-scale jet appears only for a limited set of model parameters and jet directions (see, e.g., Larchenkova et al.  ).

The main idea of the proposition is that the lensed jet images of source B0218+357 retain its proper geometric shape on scales of tens of as. According to the VLBA survey of extragalactic sources at 15 GHz the correlated flux density for B0218+357 does not decrease to zero even at the maximum projected baseline of this ground-based interferometer ( 440 10^ 6 wavelengths) but is about 250 mJy  .

sources.summary: The paper treats B2 0218+35 as a lensed high-energy source whose gamma-ray and radio delays agree, while differing magnification ratios suggest different emission-region sizes.

quotes.quote: For the second lensed source, B2 0218+35, detected in high energies, the components of delayed ray flare B2 0218+35 have similar fluxes, implying a magnification ratio close to 1 HujCheung .

Radio observations of this source give a time delay consistent with the time delay for the ray flare. The time delay for the radio images at 15 and 8.4 GHz is 10.5 0.2 days 1999MNRAS.304..349B and 10.1 0.8 days 2000ApJ...545..578C . Radio observations give much larger magnification ratios of 3.2 ^ +0.3 _ 0.4 at 8 GHz and 4.3 ^ +0.5 _ 0.8 at 15 GHz 2000ApJ...545..578C . For B2 0218+35, one can construct a consistent model where the radiation sources coincide, but differ in size.
During the flare, the size of the ray source is <6 10^ 14  cm HujCheung , much smaller than the Einstein radius. In the radio, the jet extends well beyond the Einstein radius.

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO B0218+357 as a distant gravitationally lensed blazar whose delayed VHE flare was detected by MAGIC, making it a rare EBL probe.

quotes.quote: QSO B0218+357 is a distant blazar, likely a flat spectrum radio quasar, located at the red-shift of z=0.944 and gravitationally lensed by the galaxy B0218+357 G located at the red-shift of 0.68 . Einstein's rings separated by only 335 mas have been detected in radio . Time delays between the two components have been detected in radio (of 10-12 days ) and recently in HE rays ( 11.46 0.16 days ), using auto-correlation analysis of the light curve .

In July 2014, QSO B0218+357 entered a flaring state in HE , with an intensity increase by a factor of 20 . Due to full moon period, MAGIC could not detect the original HE flare, but detected the source at the expected arrival time of the delayed flare , with a statistical significance of 5.7 . The corresponding \ and MAGIC light curves are shown in Fig. fig:MAGIC_GSOB0218 . QSO B0218+357 is, with PKS 1441+25 one of the two most distant sources (red-shift z 0.9 ) known in VHE rays, allowing to derive strong constrains on the EBL.

sources.summary: The paper identifies QSO B0218+357 as a high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar whose lensed, delayed gamma-ray flare was detected and modeled with multiwavelength data.

quotes.quote: QSO B0218+357 Even though there are already over 60 blazars detected in the very high energy (VHE, 100 GeV) range, most of them are relatively close-by sources with redshift z 0.5 . Until mid 2014, the farthest sources observed in this energy range were 3C 279 ( z=0.536 , al08 ), KUV 00311-1938 ( z>0.506 , be12 ) and PKS1424+240 ( z=0.601 , acc10 ). In the last two years the MAGIC (Major Atmospheric Gamma Imaging Cherenkov) telescopes discovered VHE gamma-ray emission from \ at z=0.944 atel6349 and afterwards PKS1441+25 at z=0.940 ah15 almost doubling the boundaries of the known gamma-ray universe.

, also known as S3 0218+35, is classified as a flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ, ac11 ). The classification is based on the optical spectrum co03 . It is located at a redshift of z_s=0.944 0.002 co03 . The object is gravitationally lensed by the face-on spiral galaxy \ located at a redshift of z_l=0.68466 0.00004 cry93 .

sources.summary: The paper discusses S3 0218+357 as a lensed TeV blazar, confirms lens absorption, detects broad Mg II, and leaves the source redshift tentative.

quotes.quote: S3 0218+357 : This source was discovered to be a gravitational lens by patnaik1993 who detected two similar radio sources with 0.33'' separation and an Einstein ring with the same diameter. Optical counterparts of the two radio-sources were observed and detected by HST images jackson2000, york2005 .

We obtain an optical spectrum ranging from 4500 to 10000 with a SNR in the range 25-50. We confirm the detection of Mg II and Ca II absorption lines at z = 0.684, and in addition we clearly detect an absorption line at 9920 identified as Na I 5892 at the redshift of the lens. We do not detect the emissions line [ OII ] , H _ and [ OIII ] claimed by cohen2003 . We clearly detect the strong broad emission line at 5480 (EW=35 , FWHM=4700 km/s) that if attributed to Mg II 2800 , yields the redshift of z = 0.954. Therefore we conclude that the redshift of the S3 0218+357 is still tentative since it is based on only one line.

sources.summary: The paper presents B0218+357 as a very distant TeV-emitting gravitationally lensed blazar whose delayed flare image was detected by MAGIC and requires two jet zones.

quotes.quote: B0218+357 Two main features characterize the FSRQ B0218+357: its large redshift, making this source the most distant TeV emitter known to date, and the fact that this source is a gravitationally lensed blazar. The lens is the spiral galaxy B0218+357G located at a redshift of z 0.68 . The radio image clearly shows two components, with a delay between the two images measured during flaring episodes of 10-12 days. This delay was also seen in 2012 at high energies by Fermi LAT during a series of outbursts .

The successive strong flare from the source was registered by Fermi in 2014, and triggered observations with the MAGIC telescopes. However MAGIC could point the source only during the expected arrival time of the delayed component of the emission. A significant signal was detected by MAGIC below 200 GeV .

sources.summary: The paper treats JVAS B0218+357 as a promising time-delay gravitational lens: an isolated single lens galaxy producing two source-core images and a structured Einstein ring used to constrain H0.

quotes.quote: The lensed B0218+357 patnaik93 is one of the rapidly growing but still small number of gravitational lens systems with an accurately known time-delay biggs99,cohen00 . It is therefore a candidate to apply Refsdal's method to determine the Hubble constant H_0 refsdal64b . Besides the time-delay and an at least partial knowledge of the other cosmological parameters, only good mass models of the lens are needed to accomplish this task.

The lens , discovered in the JVAS survey patnaik93 , meets most of the criteria for a `Golden Lens' which allows an accurate determination of H_0 . The lens is an isolated single galaxy, and no field galaxies nearby contribute to the lensing potential significantly lehar00 . The lens shows only two images of the core of the source, but substructure of the images can be used as further constraint. The main bonus is the presence of an Einstein ring which shows a lot of substructure in detailed radio maps biggs01 .

sources.summary: The paper describes B0218+357 as a molecule-rich gravitational lens with high column density, an Einstein ring, resolved quasar images, and a nearly face-on spiral lens galaxy.

quotes.quote: B0218+357 z=0.685 This system corresponds to the largest column density N(H _2 ) 10^ 24 cm ^ 2 ; all three CO isotopes (CO, ^ 13 CO and C ^ 18 O) have optically thick lines, and this allowed the detection of many molecules (Combes Wiklind 1997). It was for instance possible to search for molecular oxygen O _2 without atmospheric absorption, with the redshifted lines at 368 and 424 GHz: the abundance ratio was constrained to O _2 /CO < 2 10 ^ 3 , meaning that most of the oxygen should be in atomic form (Combes Wiklind 1995, Combes et al 1997). The system is a gravitational lens, with two images of the quasar, and an Einstein ring.

The image separation is 335mas (1.8kpc), well determined through radio interferometry (VLA, MERLIN, Browne et al 1993). Substructures have been resolved in each of the two images, in the form of an extended radio jet component and two bright cores, through VLBI at 8.4 GHz, with 1mas synthetized beam (Biggs et al 2003). The lens is a spiral galaxy almost face-on, and spiral arms complicate the lens analysis, to obtain the Hubble constant value from the time delay (York et al 2005).

sources.summary: The paper describes B0218+357 as a gravitationally lensed BL Lac with an Einstein ring, variable compact images, jets, and molecular absorption in the lensing spiral.

quotes.quote: B0218+357 \ is a gravitational lens with an Einstein ring. The source is a BL Lac object at a redshift z 0.94, and appears, at arcsecond resolution, as two distinct point sources in the ring, with a separation of 335 mas, and a weak jet called the ``hot spot'' patnaik93,odea92,wiklind95,biggs03,cohen03 . The point sources, A B, have a flat spectrum, while the Einstein ring has a steep spectrum. They are highly variable on a timescale of a few days and each is separated into at least two subcomponents separated by a few mas biggs01,patnaik95 . The A source is brighter by a factor of 3 in the radio and both have jets wiklind95,biggs01 .
The lensing source is a spiral galaxy at a redshift of z 0.685 patnaik95, wiklind95 .

sources.summary: The paper reports LAT gamma-ray flares from lensed blazar B0218+357, measuring an 11.46-day gravitational-lens delay and noting its radio discovery, redshift, and compact double imaging.

quotes.quote: B0218+357 was discovered with the NRAO 140-ft telescope in its strong source survey S3 0218+35; pau72 . Later radio imaging revealed it to be a gravitationally lensed blazar with the smallest separation double-image known (335 milli-arcseconds), and an Einstein ring with a similar angular diameter ode92,pat93 . The lens galaxy is at redshift z=0.6847 bro93 , and the blazar was later securely measured at z=0.944 0.002 coh03 . Shortly after the lens discovery, cor96 measured a time delay ref64 =12 3 days ( 1 quoted throughout unless otherwise specified) at radio wavelengths, using the VLA to spatially separate and monitor the polarization variability in its leading brighter A (western) and fainter B (eastern) images.
Our detection of a gravitational lens time delay, =11.46 0.16 days, in the LAT observations of blazar B0218+357 has some interesting potential implications for future \ studies.
The case of B0218+357 appears to be the first clear case of a \ detected gravitational lens time delay for any astrophysical system.

sources.summary: The paper describes B0218 + 357 as a Fermi-detected lensed blazar, a double-image system with an extended jet, modeled magnifications, and the strongest ANTARES neutrino-power limit.

quotes.quote: Candidate Distant Lensed Blazars The most interesting lensed blazar candidates are B0218 + 357 1972AJ.....77..797P and PKS 1830 211 1988MNRAS : they are visible to ANTARES and have also been detected by Fermi . B0218 + 357 is a double-image lens system with an extended jet at redshift z=0.96 , while the intervening lensing spiral galaxy has z=0.68 1992AJ....104.1320O . We obtain magnification values of 12.3 and 8.5 for the two images based on the SIE model. These estimates are consistent with the observed flux ratios at radio wavelengths Biggs:1998ez . B0218 + 357 is also the first lensed system for which a clear ray measurement of a time delay for two images has been reported Cheung:2014dma .

The strongest limit is obtained for the Fermi detected B0218 + 357; it corresponds to a total neutrino power of 1.1 10^ 46 erg s ^ 1 , about one order of magnitude lower than the lowest limit achieved with non-lensed FSRQs.

sources.summary: The paper describes S3 0218+35 as a z=0.944 FSRQ/blazar gravitationally lensed by a z=0.68 galaxy, with compact radio images, an Einstein ring, and radio time delays.

quotes.quote: , also known as S3 0218+35, is a blazar, most probably a flat spectrum radio quasar (FSRQ), located at the redshift of 0.944 li12 . The object is gravitationally lensed by a galaxy \ located at the redshift of 0.68 https://www.cfa.harvard.edu/castles/ . The radio image shows two distinct components with the angular separation of only 335 mas and an Einstein's ring od92 .

Observations of variability of the two radio components led to a measurement of a delay of 10-12 days co96,bi99 . The delayed component had a 3.57-3.73 times weaker flux bi99 , however at lower radio frequencies the ratio decreases to 2.6 mi06 . The difference can be explained to occur due to additional free-free absorption in the line of sight to one of the radio images mi07 .

sources.summary: The paper investigates B 0218+357 via dual-frequency monitoring of its lensed images, describing the source as a blazar lensed by an intervening spiral and modeling observed flux-ratio variations with jet opacity.

quotes.quote: As an example, we investigate the variability of the flux-density ratio between the two lensed images of the blazar B 0218+357, using dual-frequency cm-wave observations.

The blazar 218 (located at z =0.944 0.002, coh03 ) shares many similarities with 1830. It is lensed by an intervening ( z =0.68, bro93,car93,wik95 ) spiral ( yor05 ) galaxy, which forms two bright and compact images (hereafter A and B) separated by 0.3 '' , with image B embedded in a faint Einstein ring ( odea92,pat93 ).

As an example of the advantages of using gravitational lenses in the study of weak AGN variability, we have investigated the frequency-dependent variation in the flux-density ratio between the two images of the lensed blazar 218. We used previously published dual-frequency radio monitoring data by . We showed that the observed flux-density variations can be consistently explained by a simple model of a travelling jet feature plus opacity effects in the blazar jet.

sources.summary: The paper measures the gravitationally lensed active nucleus 0218+357, deriving a time delay, variable-component flux ratios, and constraints affected by lens-galaxy position uncertainty.

quotes.quote: High precision VLA flux density measurements for the lensed images of 0218+357 yield a time delay of 10.1^ +1.5 _ 1.6 days (95 confidence). This is consistent with independent measurements carried out at the same epoch ( ), lending confidence in the robustness of the time delay measurement. However, since both measurements make use of the same features in the light curves, it is possible that the effects of unmodelled processes, such as scintillation or microlensing, are biasing both time delay measurements in the same way.

We also measure the flux ratio of the variable components of 0218+357, a measurement of a small region that should more closely represent the true lens magnification ratio. We find ratios of 3.2^ +0.3 _ 0.4 (95 confidence; 8 GHz) and 4.3^ +0.5 _ 0.8 (15 GHz). Unlike the reported flux ratios on scales of 0.1'' , these ratios are not strongly significantly different. We investigate the significance of apparent differences in the variability properties of the two images of the background active galactic nucleus.

sources.summary: The paper studies B0218+357 as a double-image gravitational lens, using phase-referenced VLBI to register lensed radio images and examine flux-ratio behavior tied to source structure.

quotes.quote: We present the results of phase-referenced VLBA+Effelsberg observations at five frequencies of the gravitational lens B0218+357 to establish the precise registration of the A and B lensed image positions. The potential to determine the Hubble constant, H_ 0 , using the double image lens B0218+357 was established shortly after its discovery (Patnaik et al. 1993) to be very high. This is because of the accurately measured value for the time delay between the images, (10.5 0.4) d (Biggs et al. 1999) and the wealth of data coming from numerous radio and optical observations of this source at various frequencies and epochs that provide constraints for the lens model.

Yet this system presents a few `glitches'. One of them is the steady and systematic decline in the radio image flux density ratio with decreasing frequency. One of the possible explanations is a frequency-dependent source structure (the background source is conjectured to be a blazar), combined with the magnification ratio which changes significantly over the extent of the structure.

sources.summary: The paper treats 0218 + 357 as one of two quasar absorption sources, adopts its molecular redshift, and uses HI-molecular redshift agreement to limit changes in physical constants.

quotes.quote: We have compared the frequency of high-redshift Hydrogen 21 \ absorption with that of associated molecular absorption in two quasars to place new (1 sigma) upper limits on any variation in y g_p ^2 (where is the fine structure constant and g_p is the proton g-factor) of | y / y| < 5 10^ 6 at redshifts z=0.25 and z=0.68 . These quasars are separated by a comoving distance of 3000 \ (for H_0=75 and q_0=0 ).

In the case of 0218 + 357 Wiklind Combes (1995) work with respect to the HI redshift of z=0.68466 (Carilli, Rupen Yanny 1993) but it is clear from their Fig. 3 that the centre of the molecular absorption (measured from the \ system) is 4 0.5 \ higher corresponding to z=0.684680 0.000006 , the value we adopt here.

In the two sources 0218 + 357 and 1413 + 135 the redshifts agree to within our errors. (As discussed above we are not analyzing 1504 + 377.) We can therefore combine the uncertainties in quadrature to give 1-sigma upper limits on the redshift differences. These are shown in the Table and are | z / 1+z | < 5 10^ 6 (1.5 ) for both sources.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyses VLA monitoring of JVAS B0218+357, a compact gravitational lens with two images and an Einstein ring, deriving an 11.3-day delay and Hubble-constant constraint.

quotes.quote: We have reanalysed the 1996/1997 VLA monitoring data of the gravitational lens system JVAS B0218+357 to produce improved total flux density and polarization variability curves at 15, 8.4 and 5 GHz. This has been done using improved calibration techniques, accurate subtraction of the emission from the Einstein ring and careful correction of various systematic effects, especially an offset in polarization position angle that is hour-angle dependent. The variations in total and polarized flux density give the best constraints and we determine a combined delay estimate of 11.3 0.2 d (1 ).

With a separation of only 334 mas between the two lensed images (A and B) B0218+357 remains the smallest gravitational lens system detected to date and also contains an Einstein ring of approximately the same diameter. This system is well-studied, predominantly due to its suitability for measuring H_0 - both the lensing galaxy and lensed source redshifts are known browne93,cohen03 , the presence of a complete Einstein ring gives ample modelling constraints wucknitz04b and the position of the lens galaxy has been independently measured using a deep Hubble Space Telescope image york05 .

sources.summary: MA0.68 is a z=0.68466 nearly face-on spiral lens with one molecular absorption sightline, intermediate H2 column density, and possibly higher intrinsic metallicity than MA0.89.

quotes.quote: The second molecular absorber (hereafter labeled MA0.68), located in front of the quasar 0218, shares similar properties with MA0.89. It is a nearly face-on spiral galaxy at an intermediate redshift z=0.68466, also lensing the background quasar into two main compact images and an Einstein ring, seen at low radio frequencies and centered on the faintest image. Molecular absorption has only been detected in one line of sight (toward the brightest image), at an impact parameter of 2 kpc from the absorber's center Wiklind1995, Muller2007 . A number of molecules and their isotopologs have also been observed in MA0.68 e.g. Wallstrom2016 , which has an H _2 column density ( 8 10^ 21 cm ^ 2 ) that is intermediate between MA0.89 SW and MA0.89 NE.
The simplest explanation of the difference in chlorine isotopic ratios between MA0.89 and MA0.68 might be that MA0.68 has an intrinsically higher metallicity.

sources.summary: The paper describes QSO B0218+357 as a high-redshift flat-spectrum radio quasar lensed by a foreground spiral galaxy and detected by MAGIC during a violent flare.

quotes.quote: Gravitational lensed rays QSO B0218+357 is a flat spectrum radio quasar located at a redshift of z = 0.944 that is gravitationally lensed by the spiral galaxy B0218+357 located at z = 0.685. Due to the gravitational effect of the intervening galaxy B0218+357, the photons emitted from QSO B0218+357 in the direction to the observer form two paths that reach the observer with a delay of about 11 days . In July 2014 QSO B0218+357 experienced a violent flare observed by the Fermi LAT and followed by the MAGIC telescopes. This is the farthest VHE source detected to date, achieving a flux of 30 of the Crab Nebula at 100 GeV . The spectral energy distribution of QSO B0218+357 is consistent with current EBL models.

sources.summary: The paper presents 8.4 GHz global VLBI maps of JVAS B0218+357, resolving mas-scale jet substructure, constraining the lens mass profile, and finding scattering/depolarization from the lensing galaxy.

quotes.quote: We have presented new maps of the lens system JVAS B0218+357 made with a global VLBI array that combine excellent sensitivity and resolution to produce the best maps yet of the mas-scale structure of this system. Unfortunately, we were unsuccessful in detecting the third image or the core of the lensing galaxy. With the new maps we have been able to explore several aspects of the lensing galaxy. From the different lengths of the jet in each image we have been able to constrain the radial mass profile and find that it is slightly steeper than isothermal ( 1.04 ). We also find evidence for multi-path scattering and depolarisation due to the disparity between the component sizes and polarisations in each image.

sources.summary: The paper describes B0218+357 as a gravitationally lensed absorber with two main images, an Einstein ring, high column density, optically thick CO isotopic lines, and rich molecular searches.

quotes.quote: Absorption in the intervening lens galaxy B0218+357 B0218+357 is amplified by a gravitational lens at z=0.685: the source is split in 2 main images A and B, with an Einstein ring (cf Figure 1). In VLBI, the A and B components reveal a detailed structure, with two bright cores and extended radio jet components (Biggs et al 2003). It is the absorber with the largest column density around 10 ^ 24 cm ^ 2 at maximum. All three CO isotopic lines up to C ^ 18 O are optically thick (Combes Wiklind 1995). This has allowed the search of many molecules, and in particular important ones undetected in our Galaxy due to atmospheric absorption at z=0.

sources.summary: The paper cites QSO B0218+357 as a lensed blazar whose time-delayed gamma-ray flare enabled prediction and VHE detection of a second image, motivating faster machine-learning flare forecasts.

quotes.quote: The measured time delay on QSO B0218+357 and a reported gamma-ray flare of this source was used to predict the occurrence of the second image of the same flare. Thus, it allowed detection of the source at the VHE gamma ray band, and observe it in a broadband observational campaign already from the onset of the flare 2016A&A...595A..98A . That study had been however marred by the lack of VHE observations of the flare in the first (earlier) image. Machine learning flux prediction method might be able to provide faster information about the expected evolution of the source, and hence would aid in catching such flares with VHE gamma-ray in multiple images, opening a possibility for probing their morphology as well as cosmology 2018PhR...778....1B .

sources.summary: The paper treats B0218+357 as a special lensing system whose BL Lac source likely has a jet pointed toward us, yielding unusually small or even blueshifted intrinsic redshift and affecting H0 estimates.

quotes.quote: The source in the system B0218+357 is special. It is a BL Lac object. The jet from this BL Lac source appears to be pointing at us, and we are then looking at the head of the jet with a smaller intrinsic shift than a regular quasar. The shift may even be a small intrinsic blue shift rather than a small redshift.

sources.summary: The paper describes JVAS B0218+357 as a doubly imaged BL Lac lens with unusually small image separation and a gas-rich late-type lens galaxy.

quotes.quote: JVAS B0218+357 (a) . This system is a doubly imaged blazar (BL Lac type), first considered as such in Patnaik1993 by Patnaik1993 in the context of the Jodrell Bank/VLA Astrometric Survey (JVAS). It has the smallest image separation found so far in lensed quasar systems. The lens is a gas-rich late-type galaxy Keeton1998 .

sources.summary: The paper measures VLA 8 and 15 GHz time delays for JVAS B0218+357, combines them to 10.7 days, and notes agreement with earlier monitoring.

quotes.quote: - JVAS B0218+357: From 8 GHz and 15 GHz VLA observations reported by Cohen2000 , we measured time delays of 10.4 1.0 days and 11.4 1.5 days, respectively. Taking the weighted average of the two results, we find the time delay to be 10.7 0.8 days. We note here that Biggs1999 monitored this system using VLA during the same period as Cohen2000 at the same two frequencies and report a time delay of 10.5 0.4 days (95 CI).

sources.original_name: z=0.69 QAS toward the Quasar TXS 0218+357
sources.summary: The paper describes the z=0.69 TXS 0218+357 absorber as a face-on spiral-galaxy QAS with molecular, 21-cm, 10 micron, and 18 micron absorption but no compelling crystallinity evidence.

quotes.quote: Similar to the z=0.89 PKS 1830-211 QAS, this absorber is in a face-on spiral galaxy, with absorption from > 10 different species of molecules including CO, HCO ^+ , H _2 O, NH _3 , and LiH, as well as 21-cm absorption. We detect both the 10 m silicate absorption feature at 10.7 (in EW), and additionally detect the 18 m feature at > 3 [ 9 ] . We do not find compelling evidence of silicate crystallinity, although, due to the low signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of the TXS 0218+357 spectrum, we cannot rule out crystalline silicates producing some of the smaller substructure features in the absorption profile.

sources.summary: The paper treats S4 0218+35 as a high-redshift FSRQ detected at very high energies by MAGIC, using its z=0.944 flaring detection as agreement with blazar survey predictions.

quotes.quote: Our results imply that 100 new blazars can be detected now at very high energy and up to z 1 , consistently with the very recent MAGIC detection of S4 0218+35 at z=0.944 .
Very recently MAGIC has detected VHE photons from S4 0218+35, an FSRQ at z=0.944 , during a flaring event Mirz_2014 .

The very recent VHE detection of S4 0218+35 at z=0.944 by MAGIC is in full agreement with this prediction; the fraction of BL Lacs with redshift increases when the effect of the EBL is taken into account from 70 - 80 to 90 .

sources.summary: The paper identifies S4 0218+35 as a gravitationally lensed blazar whose waiting-time distribution clusters near an 11-day delayed echo seen in radio and gamma-ray data.

quotes.quote: In fact, there is an hint of a complex structure from the 12h binned light curve reported in dammando2019 . a peculiar source: S4 0218+35 The sample of sources we analysed includes also S4 0218+35, a blazar with a gravitational lens along the line of sight. The effect of the gravitational lens shows up in the distribution of waiting times reported in Figure fig_waiting_times_S4_0218p35 . They cluster around _t \ 11 d (observer frame), in close agreement with the waiting time of delayed echo measured with VLA biggs1999,cohen2000 and with the gamma-ray data itself barnacka2016 . remaining sources In order to reduce the signal from the short component, we performed a further investigation of waiting times distribution removing CTA 102 from the sample.

sources.summary: The paper uses the lensed blazar B0218+357 to test magnetic-field effects on gamma-ray lensing, but finds only weak limits from its configuration.

quotes.quote: This method is demonstrated by the example of the blazar B0218+357. In this case however it is not possible to obtain useful constraints due to the large distances to the blazar and the lens galaxy. The result is only a lower limit on the magnetic field B>2 10^ 17  G, which is weaker than other existing constraints.

gmw_00000450.name: HDF 850.1
gmw_00000450.overview: HDF 850.1 is a dust-obscured, ultraluminous infrared starburst galaxy at redshift z = 5.183 [arXiv:1808.04372, arXiv:1401.2396]. It consists of two merging primordial components [arXiv:1401.2396] and is weakly lensed by the foreground elliptical galaxy 3-586.0 [arXiv:1401.2396, arXiv:1409.0558].

sources.summary: The paper models HDF 850.1 as a submillimeter galaxy whose photometric redshift could be raised substantially by far-infrared ice emission, though it does not prefer the most extreme model.

quotes.quote: We then take the submillimeter galaxy HDF 850.1 as an example to show how FIR ice emission allows a very large redshift estimate. Absorption Observations of strong absorption features from refractory dust in ULIRGs trace dust that is cooler than the temperature of a blackbody emitting at the wavelength of the feature: 300 K for the 10 m silicate feature and 150 K for the 20 m silicate feature for example. Thus, 20 m silicate features are weak in ULIRGs compared to 10 m features owing to the dust radial temperature gradient.

Modeling ( aretxaga ) of these observations leads them to conclude that the redshift of HDF 850.1 lies in the range 4.1 0.5. Since the SED of a high q ULIRG (IRAS 08572+3915) is included in the 20 templates in the model input, the effects of the change in the radio-FIR relation for ULIRGs compared to lower luminosity galaxies noted in the introduction should be partly represented in their estimate. Similarly, the suggestion of ice emission in the SED of IRAS 14348-1447 (Fig. 3) could also influence the estimate if the photometric data were used directly rather than fitted with a modified blackbody.

We've considered two models where the presence of far-infrared emission features owing to ice in the SED of HDF 850.1 would place it at redshifts larger than the range estimated without ice by . In the first, the use of crystalline ice at 150 m as a substitute for amorphous ice is probably justified, subject to further laboratory investigations of amorphous ice.

sources.summary: The paper models compact elliptical galaxy 3-586.0 as a weak gravitational lens for HDF 850.1, estimating its lensing magnifications and effects on the observed merger components.

quotes.quote: The C ^+ line contours in Fig. 1 are superposed on a greyscale version (Downes et al. \ 1999) of the BVI image from the Hubble Deep Field, showing, as has been known for some time, that there are no optical counterparts at the positions of the merger galaxies in HDF 850.1. This superposition also shows clearly that the lines of sight to both of the merger components of HDF 850.1 pass close to the compact elliptical galaxy 3-586.0 (Williams et al. \ 1996), so both of the merger galaxies are subject to weak gravitational lensing. With our new measured positions for the HDF 850.1 North and South galaxies, we re-computed the gravitational lensing effect by the z = 1.2 elliptical galaxy 3-586.0. We took the position of this elliptical galaxy from Barger et al. \ (2008).

We modeled the lens as a singular isothermal sphere, at redshift z = 1.224 (Barger et al. \ 2008), with velocity dispersion 150 \ (Dunlop et al. \ 2004). We assumed reasonable values for the true (undeflected) offsets of the two HDF 850.1 galaxies in R.A. \ and Dec., and slowly varied these assumed offsets until the predicted lensed images of the two galaxies fell at their observed (deflected) displacements from the elliptical. Table 2 gives the results. In this model, the lensing magnification is 1.7 for the North galaxy, and 1.5 for the South galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper presents first rest-frame optical/near-infrared detections of HDF850.1, rejects HDF850.1K as a counterpart, and estimates a massive, rapidly star-forming z=5.183 submillimetre galaxy with moderate lensing.

quotes.quote: We present rest-frame optical and near-infrared detections of one of the highest redshift submm-selected galaxies to date, HDF850.1. We do not detect the previously proposed counterpart HDF850.1K in new deep J and H band HST WFC3 data, placing a strong limit of H-K>3.8 , concluding that the K band source is spurious. However, we detect 5.8 m and 8 m emission co-located with the submm in deblended images. After modelling and removing the flux contributions from another foreground galaxy, we constrain the stellar mass of HDF850.1 to be (2.5 1) ^ 1 10^ 11 M_ for a lensing magnification =1.9 0.3 , with a specific star formation rate of 8.5 Gyr ^ 1 , faster than the 1-4 Gyr ^ 1 observed for UV selected galaxies at this epoch.

Following this, and over a decade after the original discovery of HDF850.1, Walter et al. (2012) made a breakthrough CO and [ C ii ] redshift determination of HDF850.1. This placed the system at z=5.183 , making it one of the highest confirmed redshift of any submm galaxy together with, e.g., the z=5.2 lensed submm galaxy identified by Combes et al. 2012 and the z=6.34 starburst discovered by Riechers et. al 2013. Remarkably HDF850.1 remained nonetheless undetected in all rest-frame ultraviolet, optical and near-infrared data, making it appear completely obscured despite the moderate surface density of its star formation ( 35 M_ yr ^ 1 kpc ^ 2 , uncertainty 50 ; Walter et al. 2012). In this paper we use new HST WFC3 archival near-infrared data, plus a careful re-analysis of the archival Spitzer data, to reassess these claimed optical/near-infrared non-detections as well as the claimed magnification factors in this lensed system; our key results are the first rest-frame optical/near-infrared detections of HDF850.1.

sources.summary: HDF850.1 is described as a dusty submillimeter starburst at z=5.183 anchoring a galaxy overdensity; it is UV/optically faint, dominates star formation, and may become the brightest cluster galaxy.

quotes.quote: Through the analysis of the environment around the well-known SMG HDF850.1, Walter12 discovered a galaxy overdensity physically related to this dusty starburst located at z_ CO =5.183 . This SMG is undetected even in deep HST imaging in the rest-frame UV/optical and only seen in far-infrared/submillmeter wavelength regime where the spectroscopic redshifts comes from.
Due to its faintness in the rest-frame UV/optical, no Ly emission is expected to be seen in HDF850.1, confirmed by our data.

HDF850.1 has a star-formation rate of 850 M _ per year (W12). Whereas the sum of all 12 members (without the QSO) observed by us sum up to maximal 40 M _ per year. Even applying an extinction correction the SFR of this protocluster is strongly dominated by the SMG. Within 1 ^ , we find three other rest-frame UV-bright galaxies. To summarize, we infer that the dusty starburst HDF850.1 could evolve into the brightest cluster galaxy and thus should be the center of this overdensity.

sources.summary: The paper resolves HDF850.1’s dust-obscured stellar component, derives its mass and star-formation properties, detects line emission and kinematics, and places it in a filamentary overdense environment.

quotes.quote: In this paper, we have studied the stellar component and overdense environment of HDF850.1 at z=5.18 , the brightest submillimeter galaxy in the Hubble Deep Field. By combining JWST/NIRCam 0.8--5.0 \ imaging observations obtained with JADES and 3.9--5.0 \ WFSS observation obtained with FRESCO, we detect and resolve the rest-frame optical stellar component of HDF850.1 for the first time since its discovery hughes98 . We also detect the \ line emission from HDF850.1 through grism spectroscopy. In addition, we identify 109 galaxies in the redshift range of z=5.17-5.30 , where only eight galaxies were spectroscopically confirmed previously.

After correcting for a mild lensing magnification of =1.7 , we derive the stellar mass ( (M_ star / M _ )=11.0 0.1 ), star-formation rate ( SFR / ( M _ yr ^ 1 ) =3.0 0.2 based on mid-to-far-IR SED modeling ) and dust attenuation ( A_V = 4.6 0.7 ) for HDF850.1. As one of the most massive galaxies at z>5 , most of the star-formation of HDF850.1 is dust-obscured. This places HDF850.1 at the massive end of the star-forming main sequence at z 5 . After correcting for dust attenuation, we find that the morphology of HDF850.1 can be well described by a single galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper discusses HDF850.1 as an SCUBA source whose counterpart could be WFPC2 3-586, 3-593, an obscured galaxy, or source 1277, with 3-593 favored among visible sources.

quotes.quote: VLA observations by dwn99 indicate that the galaxy corresponding to HDF850.1 is either WFPC2 3-586 or 3-593 if it is associated with an optically visible galaxy. It could also be an optically obscured galaxy lying between the two galaxies. Examination of the F160W image does not reveal an optically undetected galaxy lying between the sources, however, the images of the two galaxies slightly overlap at a level below the threshold set for positive detection in sec-dr .

Of the two visible sources 3-593 (1212) is by far the better candidate for HDF850.1. An alternative source is 1277 which lies closer than 1 to the position of HDF850.1 given by hug98 . It is barely visible in the F814W band but is a strong source in the F110W and F160W bands. It is a high redshift object (z = 4.8) with a extinction of E(B-V) = 0.3 and a very late template of 5.9. Source 1277 has a luminosity of 1.49 10^ 11 L _ and a predicted 850 flux of 0.125 mJy which is also below the observed flux.

sources.summary: The paper identifies 3-593, source 1212, as a very late, extincted galaxy at z=1.84 and the better visible candidate for HDF850.1 despite its underpredicted 850 micron flux.

quotes.quote: WFPC2 3-593.0 and 3-593.2 correspond to source 1212 in Table tab1 . This is a very late galaxy (Template 6.0) with an E(B-V) of 0.4 at a redshift of 1.84. It has a luminosity of 1.76 10^ 11 but the predicted 850 flux is only 0.16 mJy, below the detected flux of 7 mJy. This is a case where the available parameter space of the analysis may have been inadequate to identify this object as the SCUBA source. The analysis picked the hottest SED with a relatively high E(B-V).

If an even hotter SED were available it might have given a better fit with a correspondingly higher E(B-V). The combination of the hotter SED and higher E(B-V) could dramaticly increase the expected 850 flux. Of the two visible sources 3-593 (1212) is by far the better candidate for HDF850.1.

sources.summary: The paper treats GN14/HDF850.1 as a complicated secure submillimeter identification affected by foreground ellipticals, possible lensing, and only upper limits to Spitzer fluxes.

quotes.quote: GN14: This source is also known as HDF850.1, and is discussed in detail in several papers (see Dunlop et al. 2004, and references therein). There is a low redshift ( z_ spec =0.300 ), radio detected galaxy 6 arcseconds to the south-west of the submm position with a P value of 0.04. While this would qualify as a secure counterpert under our criteria, there is additional evidence which suggests that the counterpart for GN14 is much more complicated. Due to a detection with IRAM and MERLIN, the counterpart to GN14 is thought to lie behind a foreground elliptical galaxy very near the submm position. In the new reduction of the VLA data, we find a 2.7 peak at the position of the Dunlop et al. (2004) counterpart.

There is also an additional complication from the foreground elliptical galaxy possibly lensing the submm system. Nevertheless, the probability of randomly finding a red IRAC source which is also detected at 24 m this close to the submm position is <0.05 . The faintness of the MIPS flux is consistent with the photometric redshift for the submm source being 4 (Dunlop et al. 2004).

sources.summary: High-resolution C+ and dust maps show HDF 850.1 as two merging high-redshift components whose merger-driven starburst makes it ultraluminous, with optically thick line and dust emission.

quotes.quote: New high-resolution maps with the IRAM Interferometer of the redshifted C ^+ 158 m line and the 0.98 mm dust continuum of HDF 850.1 at z = 5.185 show the source to have a blueshifted northern component and a redshifted southern component, with a projected separation of 0.3 '' , or 2 kpc. We interpret these components as primordial galaxies that are merging to form a larger galaxy. We think it is the resulting merger-driven starburst that makes HDF 850.1 an ultraluminous infrared galaxy, with L_ IR 10^ 13 . The observed line and continuum brightness temperatures and the constant line-to-continuum ratio across the source imply (1) high C ^+ line optical depth, (2) a C ^+ excitation temperature of the same order as the dust temperature, and (3) dust continuum emission that is nearly optically thick at 158 m.
